questions.question: The high paging activity is called ________.
questions.explanation: High paging activity where the system spends more time swapping pages than executing processes is called Thrashing. It occurs when physical memory is insufficient for the working set of active processes.
questions.takeaway: Thrashing = Excessive paging activity causing severe performance degradation

questions.question: The Hardware mechanism that enables a device to notify the CPU is called __________.
questions.explanation: An Interrupt is the hardware mechanism that enables a device to notify the CPU that it requires attention. The CPU pauses current execution and runs an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).
questions.takeaway: Interrupt = Hardware signal that notifies CPU to pause and handle a device event

questions.question: In the running state
options: all the processes waiting for I/O to be completed are found
all the processes waiting for the processor are found
only the process which has control of the processor is found
questions.explanation: In the running state, only the process that currently has control of the processor (CPU) is found. Ready state = waiting for CPU; Blocked/Wait state = waiting for I/O.
questions.takeaway: Running State = Only ONE process (the one with CPU control) is found here

questions.question: Which of the following is crucial time while accessing data on the disk?
questions.explanation: Seek time is the most time-consuming component of disk I/O — it is the time for the disk head to move to the correct track. Rotational latency and transfer time are secondary.
questions.takeaway: Seek Time = Most critical disk access time (head movement to track)

questions.question: Process State is a part of
questions.explanation: The kernel keeps track of each task's state using a Process Control Block (PCB), which stores process state, program counter, CPU registers, memory maps, and I/O status.
questions.takeaway: PCB = Kernel data structure for tracking process state and context

questions.question: Who is called a supervisor of computer acitvity ?
questions.explanation: The Operating System is called the supervisor of computer activity because it manages all system resources, schedules processes, handles I/O, and mediates between hardware and applications.
questions.takeaway: OS = Supervisor of computer activity (manages resources, schedules processes)

questions.question: Virtual memory is __________.
options: An illusion of extremely large main memory
A type of memory used in super computers.
questions.explanation: Virtual memory creates an illusion of an extremely large main memory by using secondary storage to extend apparent RAM, allowing processes larger than physical memory to run.
questions.takeaway: Virtual Memory = Illusion of large main memory using secondary storage

questions.question: The kernel keeps track of the state of each task by using a data structure called __
questions.explanation: The kernel keeps track of each task's state using a Process Control Block (PCB), which stores process state, program counter, CPU registers, memory maps, and I/O status.
questions.takeaway: PCB = Kernel data structure for tracking process state and context

questions.question: Which of the following disk scheduling techniques has a drawback of starvation ?
questions.explanation: SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) disk scheduling can cause starvation because requests near the current head position are always served first, potentially starving distant requests indefinitely.
questions.takeaway: SSTF disk scheduling → drawback: Starvation of far-away requests

questions.question: A binary semaphore
options: is used only for mutual exclusion
has the values one or zero
questions.explanation: A binary semaphore has only two values: 0 and 1. It is used for mutual exclusion (only one process in critical section) and basic synchronization between processes.
questions.takeaway: Binary Semaphore = Values 0 or 1; used for mutual exclusion

questions.question: _________ page replacement alogorithm suffers from Belady's anamoly.
questions.explanation: FIFO (First-In First-Out) page replacement suffers from Belady's Anomaly — increasing frame count can paradoxically increase page faults. LRU and Optimal algorithms do not suffer from this.
questions.takeaway: FIFO page replacement = Suffers from Belady's Anomaly

questions.question: _________ is a high speed cache used to hold recently referenced page table entries a part of paged virtual memory
questions.explanation: Virtual memory creates an illusion of an extremely large main memory by using secondary storage to extend apparent RAM, allowing processes larger than physical memory to run.
questions.takeaway: Virtual Memory = Illusion of large main memory using secondary storage

questions.question: _________ does the job of allocating a process to the processor.
questions.explanation: The Dispatcher gives CPU control to processes selected by the short-term scheduler. It performs context switching, switches to user mode, and jumps to the correct program location.
questions.takeaway: Dispatcher = Allocates CPU to selected process (performs context switching)

questions.question: In interactive environments such as time-sharing systems, the primary requirement is to provide reasonably good response time and in general, to share system resources equitably. In such situations, the scheduling algorithm that is most popularly applied is ________.
questions.explanation: Round Robin scheduling is most suitable for time-sharing systems because it gives each process an equal, fixed time quantum ensuring fair CPU distribution and good interactive response.
questions.takeaway: Round Robin = Best for time-sharing; equal time quantum for all processes

questions.question: In the multi-programming environment, the main memory consisting of _________ number of process.
questions.explanation: In a multi-programming environment, main memory contains more than one process simultaneously. This allows CPU utilization to be maximized by switching to another process during I/O waits.
questions.takeaway: Multi-programming = More than one process in memory simultaneously

questions.question: In a multithreaded environment _______.
options: Main thread terminates after the termination of child threads.
Each thread is allocated with new memory from main memory.
Every process can have only one thread.
questions.explanation: In a multithreaded environment, all threads of a process share the same code, data, and OS resources. Each thread has its own stack, program counter, and registers.
questions.takeaway: Multithreaded environment = Threads share code, data, and OS resources; each thread has its own stack and registers

questions.question: Which of the following statement is not true?
questions.explanation: 'Not true' about sockets: sockets are NOT exclusive to TCP/IP; they support multiple protocols including UDP, Unix domain sockets, and others.
questions.takeaway: Sockets support multiple protocols, not just TCP/IP

questions.question: In one of the deadlock prevention methods, impose a total ordering of all resource types, and require that each process requests resources in an increasing order of enumeration. This voilates the _______________ condition of deadlock
questions.explanation: A deadlock occurs when a set of processes are each waiting for a resource held by another process in the set, creating a circular wait condition. Four conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
questions.takeaway: Deadlock = Circular wait; requires 4 conditions: ME + H&W + No Preempt + Circular Wait

questions.question: In the ___________ method of data transfer, the participation of the processor is eliminated during data transfer.
questions.explanation: In the interrupt-driven method of data transfer, the CPU is freed to do other work while the I/O operation proceeds, and the device interrupts the CPU when the transfer is complete.
questions.takeaway: Interrupt-driven I/O = CPU freed during transfer; device interrupts CPU when done

questions.question: A thread is a __________ process .
questions.explanation: A thread is a lightweight process — it is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Threads share memory and resources of the parent process.
questions.takeaway: Thread = Lightweight process; smallest execution unit; shares memory with parent process

questions.question: Data reside in file on disk under DOS environment, which of the following file name is invalid ?
questions.explanation: Under DOS, data in a file resides on disk and is accessed through the file system. Files are structured as sequences of bytes stored in clusters on the disk.
questions.takeaway: DOS files: data resides on disk, accessed via file system, stored in clusters

questions.question: In Priority Scheduling a priority number (integer) is associated with each process. The CPU is allocated to the process with the highest priority (smallest integer = highest priority). The problem of, Starvation ? low priority processes may never execute, is resolved by __________.
questions.explanation: The exec() family of system calls replaces the current process image with a new program. After exec(), the calling process runs the new program with the same PID.
questions.takeaway: exec() = Replaces current process image with new program (same PID)

questions.question: CPU Scheduling is the basis of _________ operating system
questions.explanation: The Operating System is called the supervisor of computer activity because it manages all system resources, schedules processes, handles I/O, and mediates between hardware and applications.
questions.takeaway: OS = Supervisor of computer activity (manages resources, schedules processes)

questions.question: A major problem with priority scheduling is _________.
questions.explanation: A major problem with priority scheduling is starvation — low-priority processes may never execute if high-priority processes keep arriving. Aging is the solution.
questions.takeaway: Priority scheduling drawback = Starvation of low-priority processes; solved by aging

questions.question: ________ scheduler selects the jobs from the pool of jobs and loads into the ready queue.
questions.explanation: The long-term (job) scheduler selects jobs from the job pool (disk) and loads them into main memory for execution, controlling the degree of multiprogramming.
questions.takeaway: Long-term scheduler = Selects jobs from pool and loads into memory; controls multiprogramming degree

questions.question: Which directory implementation is used in most Operating System?
questions.explanation: The Operating System is called the supervisor of computer activity because it manages all system resources, schedules processes, handles I/O, and mediates between hardware and applications.
questions.takeaway: OS = Supervisor of computer activity (manages resources, schedules processes)

questions.question: Saving the state of the old process and loading the saved state of the new process is called ________.
questions.explanation: A context switch saves the state (registers, PC, memory maps) of the currently running process and restores the state of the next scheduled process, allowing the CPU to switch between processes.
questions.takeaway: Context Switch = Save current process state → restore next process state

questions.question: The term " Operating System " means ________.
options: The way a floppy disk drive operates
A set of programs which controls computer working
The way a computer operator works
Conversion of high-level language in to machine level language
questions.explanation: The Operating System is called the supervisor of computer activity because it manages all system resources, schedules processes, handles I/O, and mediates between hardware and applications.
questions.takeaway: OS = Supervisor of computer activity (manages resources, schedules processes)

questions.question: Resource locking ________.
options: Is not used for disk drives
Allows multiple tasks to simultaneously use resource
Can easily cause a dead lock condition
Forces only one task to use any resource at any time
questions.explanation: Resource locking prevents multiple processes from simultaneously accessing shared resources. It ensures mutual exclusion, preventing race conditions and data corruption.
questions.takeaway: Resource locking = Ensures mutual exclusion; prevents race conditions on shared resources

questions.question: Using Priority Scheduling algorithm, find the average waiting time for the following set of processes given with their priorities in the order: Process : Burst Time : Priority respectively . P1 : 10 : 3 , P2 : 1 : 1 , P3 : 2 : 4 , P4 : 1 : 5 , P5 : 5 : 2.

questions.takeaway: find = Search files by name, type, date, permissions; -exec runs commands on results

questions.question: Routine is not loaded until it is called. All routines are kept on disk in a relocatable load format. The main program is loaded into memory & is executed. This type of loading is called _________
questions.explanation: Dynamic loading means a routine is not loaded into memory until it is called. All routines are kept on disk; only required ones are loaded at call time. This saves memory. Dynamic linking, by contrast, links library references at load/run time (not just when the routine is called).
questions.takeaway: Dynamic loading = Routine loaded only when called (lazy loading); saves memory

questions.question: The Purpose of Co-operating Process is __________.
questions.explanation: The purpose of cooperating processes includes information sharing (shared memory/files), computation speedup (parallel execution), and convenience. All of these are valid purposes.
questions.takeaway: Cooperating processes serve: information sharing, computation speedup, and convenience — all of the above

questions.question: The kernel of the operating system remains in the primary memory because ________.
options: It controls all operations in process
questions.explanation: The Operating System is called the supervisor of computer activity because it manages all system resources, schedules processes, handles I/O, and mediates between hardware and applications.
questions.takeaway: OS = Supervisor of computer activity (manages resources, schedules processes)

questions.explanation: The -R option of rm recursively removes a directory along with all its subdirectories and files. Without -R, rm cannot remove directories.
questions.takeaway: rm -R = Recursively remove directory and all subdirectories

questions.question: Multiprogramming systems ________.
options: Are used only on large main frame computers
Execute more jobs in the same time
Are easier to develop than single programming systems
questions.explanation: The exec() family of system calls replaces the current process image with a new program. After exec(), the calling process runs the new program with the same PID.
questions.takeaway: exec() = Replaces current process image with new program (same PID)

questions.question: Under multiprogramming, turnaround time for short jobs is usually ________ and that for long jobs is slightly ___________.
questions.explanation: Under multiprogramming, turnaround time for short jobs is usually improved because the CPU is kept busy with multiple jobs, reducing overall wait time for short processes.
questions.takeaway: Multiprogramming improves turnaround time for short jobs by keeping CPU busy

questions.question: Multiprocessing ________.
options: Is completely understood by all major computer vendors
Allows the same computer to have the multiple processors
Allows multiple processes to run simultaneously
questions.explanation: Multiprocessing uses two or more CPUs within a single system to run processes simultaneously, increasing throughput and reliability compared to single-processor systems.
questions.takeaway: Multiprocessing = Multiple CPUs in one system; increases throughput and reliability

questions.question: Which is not the state of the process ?
questions.explanation: The standard process states are: New, Ready, Running, Blocked/Waiting, and Terminated. 'Privileged' is NOT a standard process state.
questions.takeaway: Standard process states: New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated — 'Privileged' is NOT one

questions.question: A set of resources' allocations such that the system can allocate resources to each process in some order, and still avoid a deadlock is called ________.
questions.explanation: A set of resource allocations from which the system can allocate resources to each process in some order and still avoid deadlock describes a safe state.
questions.takeaway: Safe state = Resource allocation where system can still satisfy all processes without deadlock

questions.question: The principle of locality of reference justifies the use of ________.
questions.explanation: The principle of locality of reference (processes tend to access nearby memory addresses repeatedly) justifies the use of cache memory to bridge speed gaps between CPU and RAM.
questions.takeaway: Locality of reference justifies cache memory — processes reuse nearby addresses repeatedly

questions.question: The technique, for sharing the time of a computer among several jobs, which switches jobs so rapidly such that each job appears to have the computer to itself, is called ________.

questions.takeaway: Time-sharing = Divides CPU time into quanta shared among jobs; gives illusion of simultaneous execution

questions.question: In a virtural memory environment
options: slow down the computer system considerable
segmentation and page tables are stored in the RAM
segmentation and page tables are stored in the cache and do not add any substantial overhead
questions.explanation: Memory segmentation divides memory into variable-length segments corresponding to logical program units (code, data, stack). Each segment has a base address and limit stored in the segment table.

questions.question: Situations where two or more processes are reading or writing some shared data and the final results depends on the order of usage of the shared data, are called ________.
questions.explanation: A race condition is when two or more processes read/write shared data concurrently and the outcome depends on execution order. It is a critical section problem.
questions.takeaway: Race condition = Concurrent read/write of shared data with order-dependent outcome; requires synchronization

questions.question: When two or more processes attempt to access the same resource a _________ occurs.
questions.explanation: A race condition occurs when multiple processes/threads access shared data concurrently and the outcome depends on the order of execution, leading to unpredictable results.
questions.takeaway: Race Condition = Concurrent access to shared data with order-dependent results

questions.question: Which technique was introduced because a single job could not keep both the CPU and the I/O devices busy?
questions.explanation: Multiprogramming was introduced because a single job could not keep the CPU busy all the time (waiting for I/O). Multiple jobs in memory allow CPU to stay productive.
questions.takeaway: Multiprogramming introduced because single job couldn't keep CPU busy (I/O waits)

questions.question: _________ allocates the largest hole (free fragmant) available in the memory.

questions.question: A process is starved

if a queue is not used for scheduling
if it is permanently waiting for a resource

questions.question: The degree of Multiprogramming is controlled by

questions.question: Replace the page that will not be used for the longest period of time. This principle is adopted by ____________.

questions.question: Which of the following command is used to change the owner of a file or directory?
questions.explanation: The 'chown' command changes the user (owner) of a file or directory. 'chown username file' changes the owner; 'chown user:group file' changes both owner and group. 'chgrp' only changes the group; 'chmod' changes permissions.
questions.takeaway: chown = Change file/directory owner; chown user:group changes both owner and group

questions.question: Which of the following statement related to nice value is not true?
options: Only root user can assign a negative nice value to a process
Only root user can change the nice value
The positive nice value means less priority for a process
A normal user can only increase the nice value to a higher positive value
questions.explanation: 'Only root user can change the nice value' is NOT true. Normal users CAN change (increase) their own processes' nice values to higher positive numbers. Only root can assign negative nice values.
questions.takeaway: NOT TRUE: 'Only root can change nice value' — normal users CAN increase nice to positive values

questions.question: Which of the following Microsoft Exchange server role provides POP3 and IMAP4 support to users ?
questions.explanation: The Hub Transport server role in Exchange Server provides POP3 and IMAP4 protocol support for email clients to receive mail.
questions.takeaway: Exchange Hub Transport = Provides POP3 and IMAP4 client access

questions.question: In a 32 bit Linux system, the the default scope for system virtual address space is
questions.explanation: In a 32-bit Linux system with 4GB total virtual address space, by default 3GB is allocated to user space and 1GB to the kernel. So kernel (system) virtual address space = 1GB.
questions.takeaway: 32-bit Linux kernel virtual address space = 1GB (3GB user + 1GB kernel = 4GB total)

questions.question: System calls in OS enable :
questions.explanation: Applications communicate with the Linux kernel through System Calls, which are the controlled interface for requesting kernel services like file I/O, network access, and process control.
questions.takeaway: System Calls = Controlled interface between user programs and the kernel

questions.question: Page based memory management leads to
questions.explanation: External fragmentation occurs when free memory exists but is scattered in non-contiguous blocks, preventing allocation of large processes. Internal fragmentation is wasted space within allocated blocks.
questions.takeaway: External Fragmentation = Free memory scattered; Internal Fragmentation = wasted space in blocks

questions.question: Which of the following file type will be created for the hard disk device?
questions.explanation: Hard disk devices are block devices in Linux, not character devices. Block devices read/write in fixed-size blocks. Character devices (like terminals) handle data character by character.
questions.takeaway: Hard disk = Block device (reads/writes in blocks); /dev/sda is block, not character device

questions.question: Which of the following command displays the top 10 lines of a file?
questions.explanation: The top command displays real-time system performance including CPU usage, memory usage, and a list of running processes sorted by resource consumption.
questions.takeaway: top = Real-time system performance monitor (CPU, memory, processes)

questions.question: Virtual address space of a process is maintained
questions.explanation: An operating system with multiprogramming capability loads multiple independent processes into memory and switches the CPU among them to keep the CPU busy.
questions.takeaway: Multiprogramming OS = Loads multiple processes into memory; switches CPU among them

questions.question: Which of the following function correctly finds the length of a string in C?
options: int xstrlen(char *s) { int len=0; while(*s++) len++; return len; }
int xstrlen(char *s) { int len=0; while(s[len]!='\0') len++; return len; }
int xstrlen(char *s) { int len=1; while(*s++) len++; return len; }
questions.explanation: A correct custom strlen() implementation increments a counter for each character while the pointer is not at the null terminator '\0'. Options C (sizeof) returns pointer size, not string length. Option D starts len at 1, giving wrong result.
questions.takeaway: strlen equivalent: loop while *s++ (or s[i]!='\0'), incrementing counter; stops at null terminator

questions.question: Which of the following roles in not performed, by default, when the first domain controller is installed in Windows 2008 Active Directory Environment?
questions.explanation: When the first DC is installed in Windows Server 2008 AD, it automatically takes on LDAP, Kerberos, and DNS Server roles. Windows Deployment Services (WDS) is a completely separate role that must be installed manually.
questions.takeaway: First DC auto-installs: LDAP + Kerberos + DNS; WDS is a separate optional role

questions.question: Where does swap space reside?
questions.explanation: The -R option is used with chmod and chown to recursively apply permission/ownership changes to all files and subdirectories within a directory.
questions.takeaway: chmod/chown -R = Recursively apply changes to all files and subdirectories

questions.question: In a modern OS, the following memory management is used
questions.explanation: Virtual memory creates an illusion of an extremely large main memory by using secondary storage to extend apparent RAM, allowing processes larger than physical memory to run.
questions.takeaway: Virtual Memory = Illusion of large main memory using secondary storage

questions.question: An operating system with multiprogramming capability is one that________________________.
options: allows several users to use the same program at once by giving each a slice of time
runs programs over more than one processor
loads several independent processes into memory and switches the CPU from one job to
questions.explanation: An OS with multiprogramming capability loads several independent processes into memory simultaneously and switches between them when one is waiting (e.g., for I/O). This maximises CPU utilisation. Timesharing is when each user gets a CPU time slice.
questions.takeaway: Multiprogramming = Multiple processes in memory; CPU switches to keep itself busy

questions.question: An operating system is ________________.
questions.explanation: An operating system is System Software — the foundational software that manages hardware resources and provides services to application programs. It is not an application, not a CD-ROM product, and not simply integrated software.
questions.takeaway: OS = System software that manages hardware and provides services to applications

questions.question: Which of the following squid command option helps in diagnosing any problems in squid.conf file?
questions.explanation: The 'squid -X' option enables full debug output, helping diagnose problems in squid.conf. 'squid -z' initializes cache directories. 'squid -N' runs in non-daemon mode. 'squid -C' disables core dumps.
questions.takeaway: squid -X = Full debug (diagnose config problems); squid -z = Initialize cache; squid -N = No daemon mode

questions.question: Which of the following command displays the 10 most resource using services?
questions.explanation: The top command displays real-time system performance including CPU usage, memory usage, and a list of running processes sorted by resource consumption.
questions.takeaway: top = Real-time system performance monitor (CPU, memory, processes)

questions.question: Which of the following is true when a user is deleted using userdel command?
options: The group by the same user name is not deleted
When the user is deleted the home directory of the user is not deleted.
When the user is deleted all the print jobs submitted by the user are also deleted.
The files created by the user are also deleted
questions.explanation: When userdel is run without -r, the user's home directory and mail spool are NOT deleted. The primary group may be removed if no other members exist. Files remain.
questions.takeaway: userdel without -r = Home directory NOT deleted; files remain; primary group may be removed

questions.question: Virtual Memory _________________
options: allows execution of processes that may not be completely in memory
is an extremely large secondary memory
is an extremely large main memory
is a type of memory used in supercomputers
questions.explanation: Virtual memory allows the execution of processes that may not be completely loaded in physical memory at any one time. Pages are loaded on demand (demand paging). This allows programs larger than physical RAM to run.
questions.takeaway: Virtual memory = Allows processes larger than RAM to run via demand paging

questions.question: In a 64 bit Linux system, the max. scope for system virtual address space is
questions.explanation: In a 64-bit Linux system, the kernel virtual address space is far larger than any option listed (typically 128TB or more). None of the options (1GB, 3GB, 4GB) are correct.
questions.takeaway: 64-bit Linux kernel space >> 4GB (typically 128TB); none of the listed options (1/3/4 GB) is correct

questions.question: Which of the following step is not part of the Data Recovery Agent (DRA) creation process?
options: Using secpol.msc command EFS policy is defined and user is added as DRA.
Using certmanager.msc the certificated is imported.
The user account of the DRA is added to the Power Users Group.

questions.question: Which of the following file provides username and password based through apache web server? page directory
questions.explanation: The '.htaccess' file is a directory-level configuration file used by Apache to enable username/password based (Basic) authentication. It references an .htpasswd file with 'AuthUserFile'. The main httpd.conf can also set global auth but .htaccess is the per-directory mechanism.
questions.takeaway: .htaccess = Per-directory Apache config for username/password authentication

questions.question: Which option of ls command is used to display hidden files also?
questions.explanation: The '-a' option of the ls command displays ALL files including hidden files (dot files whose names start with '.'). For example, 'ls -a' shows .bashrc, .profile etc. '-l' shows long listing; '-h' shows human-readable sizes.
questions.takeaway: ls -a = Show ALL files including hidden dot files (e.g., .bashrc, .profile)

questions.question: Which of the following bit allows only owners to delete their files in a directory?
questions.explanation: The sticky bit (chmod +t) allows only the file owner to delete their files in a shared directory. This is commonly applied to /tmp.
questions.takeaway: Sticky bit = Only owner can delete their files in shared directory (e.g., /tmp)

questions.question: Which of the following protocol support is added by the samba service on Linux machine so that can share files and printers with Windows machines? it
questions.explanation: Samba adds SMB/CIFS protocol support to Linux, allowing Linux systems to share files and printers with Windows machines on a network. It implements the SMB (Server Message Block) protocol natively used by Windows.
questions.takeaway: Samba = Adds SMB/CIFS protocol to Linux for Windows file/printer sharing

questions.question: Which of the following facility will help you in putting restrictions on the use of external Windows 2008 Environment? drives in
questions.explanation: Disk quotas help put restrictions on the amount of disk space users can consume. They limit both the number of files (inodes) and disk space per user.
questions.takeaway: Disk quota = Restricts disk space and inode usage per user

questions.question: Which of the following command displays the 10 processes that are consuming maximum resources?
questions.explanation: The 'top' command displays running processes sorted by CPU and memory usage in real time, showing the top consumers by default. 'ps' shows a static snapshot; 'pstree' shows a process hierarchy tree.
questions.takeaway: top = Real-time process monitor sorted by CPU/memory; shows top resource consumers

questions.question: Which environment variable holds list of directories which can hold external commands?
questions.explanation: The env command displays all environment variables or runs a command in a modified environment. 'printenv' also shows environment variables.
questions.takeaway: env = Display/modify environment variables

questions.question: In a pre emptive OS, a process may be pre empted ?
questions.explanation: In a preemptive OS, a process running in user space can be preempted by the scheduler when its time quantum expires or a higher-priority process arrives.
questions.takeaway: Preemptive OS = Process CAN be preempted in user space when time quantum expires

questions.question: Which of the following option of sort command is used for numeric sort?
questions.explanation: The '-n' option of the sort command performs a numeric sort, treating fields as numbers rather than strings. Without -n, '10' sorts before '9' (string order). '-d' is dictionary order; '-i' ignores non-printable chars.
questions.takeaway: sort -n = Numeric sort; without -n, numbers sort lexicographically (10 < 9 is wrong)

questions.question: Which of the following command will update the group policy settings?
questions.explanation: Group Policy Objects (GPOs) are linked to AD sites, domains, or OUs to configure user and computer settings centrally. Settings are applied at login/startup and refreshed periodically.
questions.takeaway: GPO = Group Policy Object; centrally configure user/computer settings via AD

questions.question: Which of the following is not a necessary condition for a deadlock?
questions.explanation: All four Coffman conditions are NECESSARY for deadlock: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait. Since all three listed options (Mutual Exclusion, Circular Wait, No Preemption) ARE necessary conditions, 'None of the above' is the correct answer to 'which is NOT necessary'.
questions.takeaway: 4 deadlock conditions (ALL necessary): Mutual Exclusion, Hold-Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait

questions.question: Which of the following DHCP option is required to be configured to support PXE Windows Deployment Server? boot client for
questions.explanation: The default DHCP IP address lease period for wireless networks is typically 8 days. This is longer than wired networks to reduce DHCP chatter on wireless infrastructure.
questions.takeaway: Default DHCP wireless lease = 8 days

questions.question: In a non pre emptive OS, a process may be pre empted
questions.explanation: In a non-preemptive OS, a process running in user space is NOT preempted — it runs until it voluntarily yields, does I/O, or terminates. However some implementations allow system-space preemption.
questions.takeaway: Non-preemptive OS = Process in user space runs until voluntary yield or I/O; not preempted by scheduler

questions.question: Linux OS is a
questions.explanation: Linux is a monolithic OS — the entire OS kernel runs as a single program in kernel mode, including device drivers, file system, and memory management as one large kernel.
questions.takeaway: Linux = Monolithic OS; entire kernel runs as single privileged program

questions.question: Which of the following shell variable gives information about the successful execution of the previous command?
questions.explanation: The $? shell variable holds the exit status of the last executed command. A value of 0 means the command executed successfully; any non-zero value indicates an error.
questions.takeaway: $? = Exit status of last command (0 = success, non-zero = failure)

questions.question: What is the default permission given to a file created by the normal (Non root) user?
questions.explanation: The default permission for a file created by a normal user is 666 (rw-rw-rw-) before umask is applied. After default umask 022, effective permission becomes 644.
questions.takeaway: Default file permission = 666 (rw-rw-rw-) before umask; after umask 022 -> 644

questions.question: Which of the following is not an operating system?
questions.explanation: SuSE (SUSE Linux), DOS, and Unix are all operating systems. 'OSD' is not a recognised operating system (it may refer to On-Screen Display, not an OS). Therefore OSD is NOT an operating system.
questions.takeaway: Not an OS: OSD; SuSE/SUSE, DOS, and Unix are all valid operating systems

questions.question: A user named Jerry is given modify NTFS permission on a folder. The folder is shared with everyone having read permission. Which of the following access will Jerry enjoy when he tries to access the folder from the same machine?
questions.explanation: NTFS Modify permission includes Read, Write, Execute, and Delete. A user with Modify inherits the Modify level — the effective permission is Modify (not promoted to Full Control).
questions.takeaway: NTFS Modify permission = Includes Read + Write + Execute + Delete (not Full Control)

questions.question: Which of the following command is used to rename a file?
questions.explanation: The 'mv' command is used to rename files in Linux. 'ls' lists files, 'rm' removes, 'cp' copies. To rename: 'mv oldname newname'.
questions.takeaway: mv = Used to rename (and move) files in Linux; ls only lists files

questions.question: Which of the following file contains user passwords in encrypted format?
questions.explanation: Encrypted user passwords in Linux are stored in /etc/shadow, which is readable only by root. /etc/passwd contains user info but not actual passwords.
questions.takeaway: /etc/shadow = Stores encrypted user passwords (root-readable only); /etc/passwd has no real passwords

questions.question: Which of the following statement is not true about the default configuration of vsftpd service?
options: All Linux users can upload files
All the users on the Linux system can access ftp service
questions.explanation: By default in vsftpd, anonymous user login IS allowed, and anonymous users CAN upload files only to designated directories. 'All users can access FTP' is NOT always true — shell access may be restricted.
questions.takeaway: Default FTP: anonymous login allowed; upload requires explicit config; not all system users have FTP access

questions.question: Segment based memory management leads to
questions.explanation: Segment-based memory management leads to external fragmentation because segments are variable-length. Free memory between segments creates scattered gaps that may be too small for new segments.
questions.takeaway: Segmentation → External Fragmentation (variable-length segments leave gaps)

questions.question: Which of the following command creates a blank file without input?
questions.explanation: The 'touch' command creates an empty blank file without any input. 'cat' can create files using redirection, but 'touch' is the standard command for creating blank files.

questions.question: Which of the following utility in windows 2008 provides a single management utility for administrators?

questions.question: Which of the following port is used by the HTTPS site in Windows 2008 IIS Server?

questions.question: Which of the following hard disk is not supported for Windows OS installation?

questions.question: pipe IPC mechanisms provides
questions.explanation: Pipe IPC provides uni-directional communication — data flows in one direction only (from writer to reader). Named pipes (FIFOs) also follow this model unless two pipes are used.
questions.takeaway: Pipe IPC = Uni-directional communication (one-way data flow)

questions.question: in a multithreading system
questions.explanation: In a multithreaded system, threads are the basic unit of scheduling, not processes. The OS scheduler dispatches individual threads to CPU cores for execution.
questions.takeaway: Multithreaded system = Threads (not processes) are the unit of scheduling

questions.question: default action for SIGSTOP signal is
questions.explanation: The default action for SIGSTOP is to STOP (suspend) the process. SIGSTOP cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored — the kernel always suspends the process. It is resumed by SIGCONT. SIGKILL terminates; SIGSTOP only suspends.
questions.takeaway: SIGSTOP = Unconditionally suspend process (cannot be caught/ignored); SIGCONT resumes

questions.explanation: An Interrupt is the hardware mechanism that enables a device to notify the CPU that it requires attention. The CPU pauses current execution and runs an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).
questions.takeaway: Interrupt = Hardware signal that notifies CPU to pause and handle a device event

questions.question: You have 3 nos. of 320 GB hard disks in a system. You configure RAID 5 (Data Striping with Distributed Parity) using these hard disks. What will be the effective storage size of this volume that will be used to store data?
questions.explanation: RAID 5 uses one disk's worth of capacity for parity, distributed across all disks. With 3 disks of 320 GB each: total = 3×320 = 960 GB, usable = (3-1)×320 = 640 GB. RAID 5 requires a minimum of 3 disks.
questions.takeaway: RAID 5 usable capacity = (N-1) × disk_size; 3×320GB RAID5 = 640GB usable

questions.question: swap area is
questions.explanation: Swap space (swap area) is located on the hard disk (secondary storage), not in main memory. It is used as an overflow area when physical RAM is full, at the cost of much slower access.
questions.takeaway: Swap area = On hard disk (not main memory); used when RAM is full; much slower

questions.question: SIGKILL is a
questions.explanation: SIGKILL is a non-maskable signal — it cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored by the process. The kernel enforces it directly. This makes SIGKILL the last resort for terminating unresponsive processes. SIGTERM (15) can be caught.
questions.takeaway: SIGKILL = Non-maskable; cannot be caught/blocked/ignored; kernel kills process immediately

questions.question: a semaphore object uses the following lock for its atomicity
questions.explanation: A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) allows only one thread at a time to execute a critical section. Unlike semaphores, mutexes have ownership — only the thread that locked it can unlock it.
questions.takeaway: Mutex = Binary lock with ownership; only locking thread can unlock

questions.question: semop is used for
questions.explanation: semop() is the system call used for semaphore operations (P/V operations or wait/signal). It atomically adjusts semaphore values. semctl() is used for initialization and control; semget() creates or obtains a semaphore set.
questions.takeaway: semop() = Semaphore operations (P/V / wait/signal); semctl() for init/control

questions.question: no. of threads in a process is decided by
questions.explanation: The number of threads in a process is decided by the developer (programmer) who writes the application. The system/OS provides the threading API but doesn't decide how many threads to create.
questions.takeaway: Number of threads = Decided by developer; OS provides API but developer controls count

questions.question: Which of the following directory interfaces on Red Hat Linux?

questions.takeaway: /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ = Directory for network interface config files on RHEL/CentOS

questions.question: after fork shared memory section is
questions.explanation: The fork() system call creates a new process (child) that is an exact copy of the calling process (parent). The child gets a copy of the parent's address space. fork() returns 0 to child, child PID to parent.
questions.takeaway: fork() = Creates child process as copy of parent; returns 0 to child, PID to parent

questions.question: in page based memory management, main memory is divided into

questions.question: shared memory IPC mechanism is fastest because

questions.explanation: Shared memory is the fastest IPC mechanism because processes directly read and write to a common memory region without kernel intervention. Synchronization (semaphores/mutexes) is required to prevent race conditions.
questions.takeaway: Shared Memory = Fastest IPC; processes share memory region (needs synchronization)

questions.question: virtual address-space of a process is maintained
questions.explanation: The ip command manages network interfaces, routing, and addresses. 'ip addr show' lists interfaces; 'ip route show' shows routing table. It replaces ifconfig and route.

questions.question: The attribute used to choose the type of font in HTML is ________.
questions.explanation: The 'face' attribute of the HTML <font> tag specifies the font typeface (e.g., <font face='Arial'>). The 'size' attribute controls font size and 'color' sets font color. Note: <font> is deprecated in HTML5; CSS font-family is preferred.
questions.takeaway: HTML <font face='Arial'> = choose font type; deprecated in HTML5 (use CSS font-family)

questions.question: a minor page fault involves
questions.explanation: A minor page fault occurs when a page is in memory but not mapped in the process's page table (e.g., first access to anonymous memory). It requires only a page frame allocation — no disk I/O. A major page fault requires disk I/O.
questions.takeaway: Minor page fault = Page in memory but not mapped; only needs frame allocation, no disk I/O

questions.question: Which of the following value in IIS web site field allows you to run multiple web sites on single IP address and using same port?
questions.explanation: In IIS, the 'Host Name' field (host header) allows multiple websites to run on the same IP address and port by matching the HTTP Host header of incoming requests. This is virtual hosting. 'Site Name' is just an admin label.
questions.takeaway: IIS Host Name field = Enables multiple websites on one IP via HTTP Host headers

questions.question: a binary semaphore's values are
questions.explanation: A binary semaphore has only two values: 0 and 1. It is used for mutual exclusion (only one process in critical section) and basic synchronization between processes.
questions.takeaway: Binary Semaphore = Values 0 or 1; used for mutual exclusion

questions.question: The keyword used to transfer control from a function back to the calling function is
questions.explanation: The 'return' keyword transfers control from a called function back to the calling function, optionally passing a value. 'goto' jumps to a label within the same function; 'switch' is a multi-branch selection statement.
questions.takeaway: return = Transfer control back to caller and optionally return a value

questions.question: Waitpid(2000,NULL,0) system call
questions.explanation: waitpid(2000, NULL, 0) waits for the specific child process with PID 2000 to terminate and cleans it up (reaps it). waitpid(-1, NULL, 0) waits for any child.
questions.takeaway: waitpid(pid, NULL, 0) = Waits for specific PID; waitpid(-1,NULL,0) = Any child

questions.question: Which of the following command is used to change the group ownership of a file or directory?
questions.explanation: The 'chgrp' command changes the group ownership of a file or directory. 'chgrp groupname filename' assigns the specified group. 'chown' changes the user owner, 'chmod' changes permissions, and 'groupmod' modifies group properties.
questions.takeaway: chgrp = Change group ownership of file/directory; chown changes user owner

questions.question: signal delivery is taken care by
questions.explanation: Signal delivery to a process is handled by the kernel. When a signal is pending (in the signal_pending field of the process descriptor), the kernel checks and delivers it at appropriate times (e.g., on return from a system call or interrupt handler).
questions.takeaway: Signal delivery is performed by the kernel, checking the process's pending signals

questions.explanation: The data section of a process is a private section — each process has its own copy. After fork(), data sections are duplicated (copy-on-write). Threads share the data section of their parent process.
questions.takeaway: Process data section = Private (not shared between different processes); threads share parent's data

questions.question: DEFault action for SIGSEGV signal is
questions.explanation: The default action for SIGSEGV (segmentation violation) signal is to terminate the process and generate a core dump. SIGSEGV occurs on invalid memory access.
questions.takeaway: SIGSEGV default action = Terminate process + core dump (invalid memory access signal)

questions.question: Which part of the HTML document would you find you META tag information?
questions.explanation: The META tag in HTML is placed in the <head> section of the document. It provides metadata like charset, description, keywords, and viewport settings.
questions.takeaway: META tag = Found in <head> section; provides charset, description, viewport metadata

questions.question: hw interrupts are
questions.explanation: Hardware interrupts are handled in system (kernel) space, not user space. When an interrupt occurs, the CPU switches to privileged (system) mode and executes the interrupt handler. User-space code cannot directly handle hardware interrupts.
questions.takeaway: HW interrupts handled in system/kernel space; CPU switches to privileged mode

questions.question: threads share
questions.explanation: Threads within a process share the data section (global/static variables), heap, and code segment. Each thread has its own private stack and registers.
questions.takeaway: Threads share: data section + heap + code; each thread has private stack + registers

questions.question: exec() family of library calls (in turn, use execve() system call)
options: replaces the current process image with a new program
creates a duplicate child process (same as fork)
creates a duplicate child process and executes a new program
terminates the current process and starts a new one

The PID stays the same but the code, data, and stack are replaced. It does NOT create a new process — that is fork(). A common pattern is fork() followed by exec().
questions.takeaway: exec() = Replaces current process image with new program (same PID); fork() creates new process

questions.question: _________ is additional information placed within HTML brackets that control how the tab is used.
questions.explanation: Tags (HTML markup) are additional information placed within HTML brackets that control how browsers format and display the content.

questions.question: Which of the following function is used to find the first occurrence of a given string in another string?

questions.question: in a modern Unix/Linux system, fork() system call :

questions.question: logical pages are

questions.question: Which of program? the following file streams is not opened automatically

questions.question: in a modern OS, the following memory-management is used

questions.explanation: Transfer of data between CPU and memory occurs in units of words (the CPU's natural data width). Transfer between memory and I/O typically uses bytes or DMA blocks.
questions.takeaway: CPU-memory data transfer = In word units; I/O transfer via bytes or DMA blocks

questions.question: if a process terminated using SIGKILL
options: it is forced termination (abnormal exit, exit code 137)
it is normal, but unsuccessful termination
questions.explanation: SIGKILL (signal 9) forces immediate termination of a process. It is an abnormal exit — the process cannot catch, block, or ignore it. The kernel terminates it directly. The exit code is 128+9=137. This is not a normal or graceful termination.
questions.takeaway: SIGKILL = Forced abnormal termination; cannot be caught/ignored; exit code 137

questions.question: ptes of primary page table point to
questions.explanation: Primary page table entries (PTEs) point to secondary page tables (in hierarchical/multi-level paging). Secondary PTEs point to actual physical page frames.

questions.question: HTML uses _________.

questions.explanation: HTML uses a set of pre-specified (predefined) tags defined by the HTML standard (W3C). Unlike XML, HTML does not support user-defined or custom tags — you use the standardized tag set.
questions.takeaway: HTML = Uses pre-specified (predefined) standard tags; XML allows custom tags

questions.question: Who is making the Web Standards?
questions.explanation: Web standards (HTML, CSS, XML, etc.) are defined by the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C), founded by Tim Berners-Lee. W3C maintains and publishes the official web standards specifications.
questions.takeaway: W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) = Makes web standards (HTML, CSS, XML)

questions.question: spin-locks used with semaphores use
questions.explanation: Spin-locks used with semaphores rely on atomic machine instructions (such as test-and-set or compare-and-swap) to safely check and acquire the lock without race conditions. These instructions execute as a single indivisible operation in hardware.

questions.question: ptes of secondary page-tables point to
questions.explanation: In multi-level paging, secondary page table entries (PTEs) point to actual physical page frames (RAM locations), not to other page tables.
questions.takeaway: Secondary page table PTEs -> Physical page frames (not to other page tables)

questions.question: if a process terminated using SIGSEGV
options: it is normal , but unsuccessful termination
questions.explanation: If a process is terminated using SIGSEGV, it means the process accessed invalid memory (segmentation fault). The default action is abnormal termination with core dump.

questions.question: Which of the following file along with .cer is required Recovery Agent for Encrypting File System in windows?

questions.question: Which of the following variable value gives successful execution of the previous command? you the information about the

questions.question: if a process terminated using exit(0)
options: it is normal , but unsuccessful termination
questions.explanation: When a process calls exit(0), it terminates normally with exit code 0, indicating success. The OS reclaims all resources allocated to the process.
questions.takeaway: exit(0) = Normal process termination with success code; OS reclaims all resources

questions.question: a page fault of an invalid virtual address leads to
questions.explanation: A page fault caused by an invalid virtual address (e.g., null pointer dereference or out-of-bounds access) results in a SIGSEGV (segmentation fault), terminating the process.

questions.question: shmdt is used to

questions.explanation: Shared memory is the fastest IPC mechanism because processes directly read and write to a common memory region without kernel intervention. Synchronization (semaphores/mutexes) is required to prevent race conditions.

questions.question: Ctrl-C generates
questions.explanation: Signals are software interrupts sent to a process to notify it of events (e.g., SIGTERM=terminate, SIGKILL=force kill, SIGSEGV=segfault, SIGHUP=hangup). Processes can handle, ignore, or block most signals except SIGKILL and SIGSTOP.

questions.question: id is used in
questions.explanation: The semaphore id (semid) is used as a parameter in both semop() (to perform operations on the semaphore set) and semctl() (to perform control operations like IPC_RMID or SETVAL). It is obtained from semget().

questions.question: a major page fault involves
questions.explanation: A major page fault occurs when the required page is NOT in physical memory and must be loaded from disk (swap space). This requires a disk I/O operation, making it much slower than a minor page fault.
questions.takeaway: Major page fault = Page not in RAM; requires disk I/O to load from swap

questions.question: which of the following is a conventional RTOS
questions.explanation: The ip command manages network interfaces, routing, and addresses. 'ip addr show' lists interfaces; 'ip route show' shows routing table. It replaces ifconfig and route.
questions.takeaway: ip = Modern network configuration tool; replaces ifconfig and route

questions.question: in page based memory management, virtual address-space is divided into
questions.explanation: In page-based memory management, the process virtual address space is divided into equal-sized virtual pages. These map to physical page frames in RAM (via the page table) or to page slots in swap space when not resident.

questions.question: Which environment commands? variable holds list of directories which can hold external
questions.explanation: The env command displays all environment variables or runs a command in a modified environment. 'printenv' also shows environment variables.
questions.takeaway: env = Display/modify environment variables

questions.question: Which of the following option is used for chmod or chown to recursively work on all sub directories?
questions.explanation: The -R (uppercase) option makes chmod and chown work recursively on all subdirectories and files. Example: 'chmod -R 755 /var/www'. Note: '-r' (lowercase) for rm means recursive, but chmod/chown use uppercase -R.
questions.takeaway: chmod/chown -R = Recursively apply to all subdirectories and files

questions.question: hw context is saved in
questions.explanation: When a process switches to kernel mode (via interrupt or system call), the hardware context (registers, PC, flags) is saved on the kernel (system-space) stack, not the user-space stack.
questions.takeaway: Hardware context saved in System-space (kernel) stack during mode switch

questions.question: virtual pages are
questions.explanation: Virtual pages are the fixed-size units of a process's virtual (logical) address space. They are the logical equivalent of physical memory frames.
questions.takeaway: Virtual pages = Fixed-size logical address space units; logical equivalent of physical frames

questions.question: waitpid(-1,NULL,0) system call

questions.question: stack section of a process is a
questions.explanation: The stack section of each thread/process is private — it is not shared with other processes or threads. Each thread has its own stack for local variables, function calls, and return addresses.
questions.takeaway: Stack section = Private per thread/process; not shared; stores local vars and call frames

questions.question: Which of the following NTFS permission are the minimum permissions required for a user on a folder to copy files from that folder and paste them to another folder?
questions.explanation: The minimum NTFS permissions required to read a file are 'Read' permissions — allowing the user to view file contents, attributes, and permissions.
questions.takeaway: Minimum NTFS permission to read = 'Read' (view contents, attributes, permissions)

questions.question: key is used in

questions.question: system call APIs use
questions.explanation: System call APIs use the TRAP (software interrupt) machine instruction to switch from user mode to kernel mode. Examples: INT 0x80 (x86 Linux), SYSCALL (x86-64), SVC (ARM). 'iret' returns from interrupt.

questions.question: virtual terminal is

questions.question: Which of the following iptables command will block an ftp access for a machine with the IP address 192.168.10.10. The command is to be given on the ftp server with IP address 192.168.10.251?

questions.question: null pointer access leads to
questions.explanation: Accessing a null pointer causes a CPU exception (specifically a segmentation fault / SIGSEGV on Linux). The MMU raises a protection fault because address 0 is not mapped to physical memory. The kernel delivers SIGSEGV to the process.
questions.takeaway: Null pointer access → CPU exception (segfault/SIGSEGV); address 0 is unmapped

questions.question: a page fault of a valid virtual address leads to
questions.explanation: A page fault on a valid virtual address (page is in swap/disk but not in RAM) triggers the OS to load the page from secondary storage into a free physical frame.

questions.question: An HTML _________ helps you create an HTML file by inserting HTML codes for you as you work.

questions.question: system calls in OS enable

questions.takeaway: System Calls = Controlled interface between user programs and the kernel

questions.question: pseudo terminal is

questions.question: a process semaphore object is in

questions.takeaway: Process semaphore = Resides in user-space (shared memory); faster, no kernel per operation

questions.question: Ctrl-Z generates

questions.question: shmctl is used to
options: disconnect a process and shared memory object
questions.explanation: Shared memory is the fastest IPC mechanism because processes directly read and write to a common memory region without kernel intervention. Synchronization (semaphores/mutexes) is required to prevent race conditions.
questions.takeaway: Shared Memory = Fastest IPC; processes share memory region (needs synchronization)

questions.question: fork() system call
questions.explanation: The fork() system call creates a new process (child) that is an exact copy of the calling process (parent). The child gets a copy of the parent's address space. fork() returns 0 to child, child PID to parent.
questions.takeaway: fork() = Creates child process as copy of parent; returns 0 to child, PID to parent

questions.question: How do you define font style of the text?
questions.explanation: '<text style="Arial">' is the correct definition/expansion. Understanding terminology is fundamental in OS and network administration.
questions.takeaway: <text style="Arial"> = How do you define font style of the text?

questions.question: An operating system with that________________________. multiprogramming capability is one
options: loads several independent processes into memory and switches the CPU from
allows several users to use the same program at once by giving each a
runs programs over more than one processor
questions.explanation: An OS with multiprogramming capability loads several independent processes into memory simultaneously and switches between them when one is waiting (e.g., for I/O). This maximises CPU utilisation.
questions.takeaway: Multiprogramming = Multiple processes loaded; CPU switches to keep itself busy

questions.question: What will happen if in a C program you assign a value to an array element whose subscript exceeds the size of array?
options: The element will be set to 0
The compiler would report an error
The program may crash if some important data gets overwritten
The array will be automatically extended
questions.explanation: In C, assigning a value to an array element beyond its declared bounds causes undefined behavior. The most common outcome is memory corruption or a program crash (segmentation fault) if critical data in adjacent memory is overwritten. The compiler does not always detect this at compile time.
questions.takeaway: C array out-of-bounds = Undefined behavior; typically causes crash or memory corruption

questions.question: semctl is used for
questions.explanation: semctl() is the system call used to perform control operations on semaphores, including initializing semaphore values, getting semaphore values, and removing semaphore sets.
questions.takeaway: semctl() = Semaphore control — initializes values, gets info, removes semaphore sets

questions.question: The signal sent to a process when the Ctrl-C key is pressed is
questions.explanation: When the user presses Ctrl-C, the terminal sends the SIGINT (Interrupt) signal to the foreground process group. By default, SIGINT terminates the process. Processes can catch SIGINT to perform cleanup (e.g., graceful shutdown). Ctrl-Z sends SIGTSTP; Ctrl-\ sends SIGQUIT.
questions.takeaway: Ctrl-C → SIGINT (interrupt); Ctrl-Z → SIGTSTP (stop); Ctrl-\ → SIGQUIT

questions.question: Which command is used to record a user login session in a file
questions.explanation: The 'script' command records an entire terminal session (all input and output) to a file (default: 'typescript'). Usage: 'script filename'. The recording stops when the user types 'exit' or presses Ctrl-D.
questions.takeaway: script = Record terminal session to a file; stop with 'exit' or Ctrl-D

questions.question: Which command is used to display the operating system name
questions.explanation: 'uname' displays system information including the operating system name. 'uname -s' prints the kernel/OS name (e.g., Linux). 'uname -a' prints all info (kernel name, hostname, release, version, machine). 'uname -r' shows the kernel release version.
questions.takeaway: uname = Display OS/kernel name; uname -a shows all system info; uname -r shows kernel version

questions.question: Which command is used to print a file
questions.explanation: The 'lpr' command sends a file to the printer queue in Linux/Unix. 'lpr filename' queues the file for printing. 'lpq' shows the print queue and 'lprm' removes a job from the queue.
questions.takeaway: lpr = Send file to printer; lpq = view queue; lprm = remove print job

questions.question: Using which command you find resource limits to the session?
questions.explanation: The find command searches for files based on criteria. 'find /path -name pattern' searches by name; '-type f' for files; '-mtime -7' for files modified in last 7 days; '-exec cmd {} \;' executes a command.
questions.takeaway: find = Search files by name, type, date, permissions; -exec runs commands on results

questions.question: Which option of ls command used to view file inode number
questions.explanation: The -i option of the ls command displays the inode number of each file. An inode stores all file metadata except the filename.
questions.takeaway: ls -i = Display inode number of each file

questions.question: find / -name ‘*’ will
options: List all files and directories in / directory
List all files and directories recursively starting from /
questions.explanation: This operating system or system administration question tests a specific concept. The correct option accurately reflects the described system behavior.
questions.takeaway: OS/system administration concept — refer to the specific topic for accurate details

questions.question: You forget the root password. You decide to reboot using a boot disk. What do you need to do to recover the root password?
options: Mount the root partition, edit the /etc/passwds file to remove the root password, reboot
Mount the root partition, edit the /etc/passwd file to remove the root password, reboot.
Mount the main partition, edit the /etc/passwd file to remove the root password, reboot.
questions.explanation: The mount command attaches a filesystem (from a device or network) to a directory (mount point) in the Linux directory tree, making its contents accessible.
questions.takeaway: mount = Attach filesystem to directory; umount = detach

questions.question: The previous system administrator put useless messages at login, what file do you edit to change the system message?
questions.explanation: The /etc/motd (Message of the Day) file contains text displayed to all users upon login. Administrators edit this file to communicate system news or warnings.
questions.takeaway: /etc/motd = Message of the Day file displayed at user login

questions.question: Which file does Linux use to resolve hostnames?
questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) resolves human-readable hostnames to IP addresses. It uses a hierarchical namespace with Root, TLD, and authoritative servers. Default port: 53 (TCP/UDP).
questions.takeaway: DNS = Resolves hostnames to IP addresses; port 53; hierarchical namespace

questions.question: What is bash?
options: A command to delete multiple files
questions.explanation: Bash (Bourne Again SHell) is a command shell and scripting language. It is the default shell on most Linux distributions. Users type commands at the bash prompt, and bash interprets and executes them. It is NOT a kernel call.
questions.takeaway: bash = Bourne Again SHell; default Linux command shell and scripting environment

questions.question: You attempt to remove a directory, but Linux won't allow you, what is the most likely problem?
options: You are not logged in as root
The directory name contains illegal characters
questions.explanation: 'The directory name contains illegal characters' is the correct definition/expansion. Understanding terminology is fundamental in OS and network administration.
questions.takeaway: The directory name contains illegal characters = You attempt to remove a directory, but Linux won't

questions.question: Which command creates a file system?
questions.explanation: The command 'fdisk' is used for this operation. In Linux/Unix administration, knowing the right command for each task is essential for efficient system management.
questions.takeaway: Command: fdisk → Which command creates a file system?

questions.question: The login shell is
options: The shell program that runs when the user logs in
Common shell for all the users that belong to the same group
The shell program that authenticates the user while logging in
questions.explanation: The login shell is the shell program that starts automatically when a user logs in. It reads initialization files like ~/.bash_profile or /etc/profile to configure the environment.
questions.takeaway: Login Shell = Shell started at user login; reads initialization files

questions.question: You are working in Linux when a program hits a flaw and stops running. Which command can be used to end the process?
questions.explanation: The kill command sends a signal to a running process. By default it sends SIGTERM (15) to gracefully terminate. 'kill -9' sends SIGKILL which forces immediate termination.
questions.takeaway: kill = Send signal to process; default SIGTERM (graceful); -9 = SIGKILL (forced)

questions.question: A user is logged into the Linux workstation, what is the best way to login to root from a shell prompt?
questions.explanation: The passwd command changes a user's password. Root can change any user's password with 'passwd username'. Regular users can only change their own password.
questions.takeaway: passwd = Change user password; root can change any user's password

questions.question: Which command is used to bring the background process to forground?
questions.explanation: 'fg' (foreground) brings the most recently backgrounded job to the foreground. 'bg' resumes a stopped job in the background. 'jobs' lists current background/suspended jobs. Use 'fg %jobnumber' for a specific job.
questions.takeaway: fg = Bring background process to foreground; bg = Resume stopped process in background

questions.question: The command chmod 4777 a.out
options: will set the sticky bit of a.out
will set the suid bit of a.out
will set the suid bit of a.out only if the command is issued by root
questions.explanation: chmod 4777 sets the SetUID bit (4) plus rwx for owner, group, and others (777). When executed, the program runs with the file owner's privileges, not the executor's.
questions.takeaway: chmod 4777 = SetUID + rwx all; executable runs with file owner's privileges

questions.question: wtmp and utmp files contain:
options: The user’s su and sudo attempts
questions.explanation: The utmp file records currently logged-in users. The wtmp file logs all login and logout events (login history). Commands 'who' and 'w' read utmp; 'last' reads wtmp to show login history.
questions.takeaway: utmp = Current logins; wtmp = Login/logout history; 'last' reads wtmp

questions.question: Applications communicate with kernel by using:
questions.explanation: Applications communicate with the Linux kernel through System Calls, which are the controlled interface for requesting kernel services like file I/O, network access, and process control.
questions.takeaway: System Calls = Controlled interface between user programs and the kernel

questions.question: Which of the following is considered as the super daemon in Unix?
questions.explanation: inetd (Internet Daemon) is the super daemon in Unix/Linux that listens on multiple network ports and spawns child daemons on demand for each connection.
questions.takeaway: inetd = Super daemon in Unix; listens on multiple ports; spawns child daemons on demand

questions.question: Unix is which kind of Operating System?
questions.explanation: Unix is a Multi-User, Multi-Tasking, and Multi-Process operating system. Multiple users can log in simultaneously, multiple tasks can run concurrently, and multiple processes execute at the same time. All three characteristics apply.
questions.takeaway: Unix = Multi-User + Multi-Tasking + Multi-Process operating system

questions.question: Which command changes a file’s group owner
questions.explanation: The command 'cgrp' is used for this operation. In Linux/Unix administration, knowing the right command for each task is essential for efficient system management.
questions.takeaway: Command: cgrp → Which command changes a file’s group owner

questions.question: Which command is used to display disk consumption of a specific directory
questions.explanation: The 'du' (disk usage) command displays disk consumption of a specific directory and its contents. 'du -sh /path' gives a human-readable summary.
questions.takeaway: du = Displays disk usage of directory; du -sh /path gives human-readable summary

questions.question: Which command is used to perform backup in unix?
questions.explanation: 'cpio' (copy in/out) is a standard Unix backup command used to archive files to and from a device or file. 'tar' is more common today, but 'cpio' is the traditional Unix backup utility. 'gzip' and 'zip' only compress, not archive.
questions.takeaway: cpio = Traditional Unix backup/archive utility; tar is the modern equivalent

questions.question: Which option of rm command is used to remove a directory with all its subdirectories
questions.explanation: The '-r' option of rm removes a directory and all its contents recursively. 'rm -r dirname' or 'rm -rf dirname' (force, no prompts) deletes entire directory trees.
questions.takeaway: rm -r = Remove directory and all contents recursively; -rf forces without prompts

questions.question: Which of these commands could you use to show one page of output at a time?
questions.explanation: The less command displays output one screenful at a time and supports both forward and backward navigation. 'more' only allows forward paging. grep and sed are text search/processing tools.
questions.takeaway: less = View output one page at a time (supports forward/backward navigation)

questions.question: Which of the following commands can be used to change default permissions for files and directories at the time of creation
questions.explanation: The 'umask' command changes the default permission mask for newly created files and directories. Lower umask values = higher default permissions.
questions.takeaway: umask = Sets default permission mask for new files/directories; lower value = more permissive

questions.question: Which of the following is not a valid run-level
questions.explanation: 'S' is the correct answer for this conceptual question about OS/networking. Understanding which statements are true vs false is key for exam preparation.
questions.takeaway: Key fact: S

questions.question: On Linux, initrd is a file
options: containing root file-system required during bootup
Contains root-file system and drivers required to be preloaded during bootup
Contains only scripts to be executed during bootup
questions.explanation: The exec() family of system calls replaces the current process image with a new program. After exec(), the calling process runs the new program with the same PID.
questions.takeaway: exec() = Replaces current process image with new program (same PID)

questions.question: Which is loaded into memory when system is booted?
questions.explanation: The ip command manages network interfaces, routing, and addresses. 'ip addr show' lists interfaces; 'ip route show' shows routing table. It replaces ifconfig and route.
questions.takeaway: ip = Modern network configuration tool; replaces ifconfig and route

questions.question: A user creates a link to a file file1 using the following command “ln file1 file2”. Which of the following is not true?
options: The number of links for file1 is displayed as 1
The number of links for file1 is displayed as 2
The number of links for file2 is displayed as 2
file1 and file2 have the same inode numbers
questions.explanation: The -i option of the ls command displays the inode number of each file. An inode stores all file metadata except the filename.
questions.takeaway: ls -i = Display inode number of each file

questions.question: Sticky bit can be set using following permission
questions.explanation: The sticky bit can be set using 'chmod +t' or numerically with '1' prefix (e.g., chmod 1755). It restricts file deletion in shared directories to owners only.
questions.takeaway: Sticky bit = Set with chmod +t or numeric 1xxx (e.g., 1755); restricts deletion to file owners

questions.question: nohup is used to
options: manually hang up the process after logout
automatically hang up the process after logout
questions.explanation: 'nohup' (no hang-up) runs a command immune to SIGHUP, so it continues running after the user logs out. Usage: 'nohup command &'. Output is redirected to nohup.out by default.
questions.takeaway: nohup = Run command that continues after logout (immune to SIGHUP)

questions.question: Which tool will allow you to FIND accounts that have not been used for two months?
questions.explanation: DSGET is the Active Directory command-line tool used to QUERY (find) properties of AD objects, including user accounts (e.g., last logon). DSADD creates, DSRM removes, and DSMOD modifies AD objects.
questions.takeaway: DSGET = Query/find AD object properties; DSADD=create, DSRM=remove, DSMOD=modify

questions.question: Command for creating a new user (NEO) in command prompt is
questions.explanation: 'net user' manages local user accounts from the command prompt. 'net user username password /add' creates a user; '/delete' removes; no switch shows account info.
questions.takeaway: net user = Manage local users; /add creates, /delete removes, no switch=view info

questions.question: If the subnet mask is 255.255.255.0 then we can have these many hosts in this network
questions.explanation: With subnet mask 255.255.255.0 (/24), there are 8 host bits, giving 2^8 = 256 addresses. Subtracting network and broadcast: 254 usable host addresses.
questions.takeaway: 255.255.255.0 mask = /24 = 8 host bits = 256 addresses = 254 usable hosts

questions.question: HTTP/S uses this port
questions.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) uses TCP port 80 for standard web traffic. HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses port 443. Web servers listen on port 80 by default.
questions.takeaway: HTTP = Port 80; HTTPS = Port 443

questions.question: The Two parts of Digital Certificates are
questions.explanation: A Digital Certificate contains two parts: the Public Key (shared openly) and associated identity information, signed by a Certificate Authority. The Private Key is kept secret by the owner.
questions.takeaway: Digital Certificate = Public Key + Identity Info signed by CA

questions.question: Which two ports are commonly used in FTP transmission?
questions.explanation: FTP uses two TCP ports: port 21 for the control connection (commands like USER, PASS, LIST) and port 20 for the active data connection (file transfer). In passive mode, a negotiated high port replaces port 20 for data.
questions.takeaway: FTP ports: 21 (control/commands) and 20 (data transfer in active mode)

questions.question: netsh firewall set icmpsetting type=ALL mode=enable
options: Enable Ping response to any client
Disable Ping response to any client
Disables file access for any client
questions.explanation: ping sends ICMP Echo Request packets to test network connectivity. It measures round-trip time (RTT) and packet loss. A successful ping confirms basic IP connectivity.
questions.takeaway: ping = Test network connectivity using ICMP Echo Request/Reply
