questions.question: Allow log on Locally Policy is under
questions.explanation: The 'Allow log on locally' policy is found under Security Settings → Local Policies → User Rights Assignment in Group Policy.
questions.takeaway: 'Allow Log On Locally' = Under Security Settings → Local Policies → User Rights

questions.question: Rick, a network administrator, wants to ensure that the logon/logoff scripts of all the users in the network are accessible from all the domains. In which of the following shares should he place the scripts?
questions.explanation: To ensure logon/logoff scripts run on all computers, Group Policy Objects (GPOs) must be linked at a level that applies to all machines — typically at the domain or site level.
questions.takeaway: Logon/logoff scripts for all computers = Link GPO at domain or site level

questions.question: This Command is used to join the system to Domain
questions.explanation: netdom join with /domain, /userd, and /passwordd:* (asterisk prompts for password) is the correct command to join a computer to a domain from the command prompt.
questions.takeaway: netdom join %computername% /domain:name /userd:user /passwordd:* = Join domain

questions.question: Of the following authentication methods, which is the one not used by IIS 7.0?
questions.explanation: IIS 7.0 supports Basic Authentication, Windows Authentication (NTLM/Kerberos), and Digest Authentication. 'Local Authentication' is not a standard or built-in IIS 7.0 authentication method. Anonymous authentication is also supported.
questions.takeaway: IIS 7.0 auth: Basic, Windows, Digest, Anonymous — 'Local Authentication' is not standard IIS

questions.question: Network Policy server is used to ( Choose all that apply)
questions.explanation: Network Policy Server (NPS) is used to secure the network by enforcing authentication and authorization policies. It functions as a RADIUS server to centrally manage access for VPN, wireless, and dial-up clients.
questions.takeaway: NPS (Network Policy Server) = RADIUS-based authentication/authorization for network access

questions.question: Which command displays the current working directory in Linux?
questions.explanation: pwd (Print Working Directory) displays the absolute path of the current directory. It is one of the most basic and frequently used Linux commands for navigation.
questions.takeaway: pwd = Print Working Directory (shows your current location in filesystem)

questions.question: Which command is used to create a new directory in Linux?
questions.explanation: mkdir (make directory) creates a new directory. 'mkdir -p path/to/dir' creates parent directories as needed. It is the standard command for directory creation in Linux.
questions.takeaway: mkdir = Make Directory; mkdir -p creates parent dirs too

questions.question: What does the 'cat' command do in Linux?
questions.explanation: cat (concatenate) reads and displays file contents to stdout. It can also concatenate multiple files: 'cat file1 file2 > combined'. It is one of the most commonly used Linux commands.
questions.takeaway: cat = Concatenate and display file contents

questions.question: Which command shows the last 10 lines of a file by default?
questions.explanation: tail displays the last N lines of a file (default 10). 'tail -f file' follows the file in real-time — very useful for monitoring log files. 'tail -n 20' shows last 20 lines.
questions.takeaway: tail = Show last 10 lines by default; tail -f = follow live updates

questions.question: Which command shows the first 10 lines of a file by default?
questions.explanation: head displays the first N lines of a file (default 10). 'head -n 20 file' shows the first 20 lines. It is the counterpart to the tail command.
questions.takeaway: head = Show first 10 lines by default; -n N to change count

questions.question: What is the root directory in Linux?
questions.explanation: The root directory '/' is the top-level directory in the Linux filesystem hierarchy. All other directories branch from it. /root is the home directory of the root user, which is different.
questions.takeaway: / = Root of Linux filesystem (not /root which is root user's home)

questions.question: Which command copies files in Linux?
questions.explanation: cp (copy) copies files and directories. 'cp file1 file2' copies file1 to file2. 'cp -r dir1 dir2' copies a directory recursively. mv moves (renames) files instead of copying.
questions.takeaway: cp = Copy files; cp -r for directories

questions.question: Which command moves or renames a file in Linux?
questions.explanation: mv (move) moves files from one location to another and can also rename them. 'mv oldname newname' renames a file. 'mv file /path/dir' moves the file to that directory.
questions.takeaway: mv = Move OR rename files/directories

questions.question: What does 'chmod 755 script.sh' do?
options: Gives owner rwx, group rx, others rx
questions.explanation: chmod 755 sets: Owner=7(rwx), Group=5(r-x), Others=5(r-x). This is the standard permission for executable scripts — owner can modify and run, others can only read and execute.
questions.takeaway: 755 = Owner:rwx, Group:r-x, Others:r-x (standard executable permission)

questions.question: Which file stores the list of all users in Linux?
questions.explanation: /etc/passwd stores user account information: username, UID, GID, home directory, and shell. Passwords are stored as 'x' with the actual hash in /etc/shadow.
questions.takeaway: /etc/passwd = User account info (username, UID, GID, home, shell)

questions.question: Which command shows all currently logged-in users?
questions.explanation: who displays information about users currently logged into the system, including their username, terminal, login time, and remote host. 'w' shows additional CPU/memory info.
questions.takeaway: who = Show currently logged-in users; 'w' shows more detail

questions.question: What does the 'echo' command do in Linux?
questions.explanation: echo displays a line of text or a variable's value to stdout. 'echo $PATH' shows the PATH variable. 'echo text >> file' appends text to a file. It is essential in shell scripting.
questions.takeaway: echo = Display text/variable to stdout; echo $VAR shows variable value

questions.question: Which command shows the manual (help page) for a Linux command?
questions.explanation: man (manual) displays the manual page for a command, showing its usage, options, and examples. 'man ls' shows the ls manual. Press q to quit. 'man -k keyword' searches manuals.
questions.takeaway: man = Display command manual page; man -k keyword to search

questions.question: Which command counts lines, words, and characters in a file?
questions.explanation: wc (word count) counts lines (-l), words (-w), and bytes/characters (-c) in files. 'wc -l file' counts only lines. 'cat file | wc -l' counts lines from piped output.
questions.takeaway: wc = Word Count; -l lines, -w words, -c bytes

questions.question: What symbol is used for output redirection (overwrite) in Linux?
questions.explanation: '>' redirects command output to a file, overwriting it. '>>' appends to the file. '<' redirects input from a file. '|' pipes output of one command to another.
questions.takeaway: > = Redirect output (overwrite); >> = append; | = pipe to next command

questions.question: Which directory contains system configuration files in Linux?
questions.explanation: /etc (et cetera) contains all system-wide configuration files and subdirectories. Examples: /etc/passwd, /etc/hosts, /etc/fstab, /etc/ssh/sshd_config. User-specific configs are in home directories.
questions.takeaway: /etc = System-wide configuration files directory

questions.question: Which command shows disk space usage of mounted filesystems?
questions.explanation: df (disk free) reports the amount of disk space used and available on mounted filesystems. 'df -h' shows human-readable sizes. 'du -sh dir' shows a directory's total size.
questions.takeaway: df = Disk Free (filesystem usage); du = Disk Usage (directory sizes)

questions.question: What is the default shell for most Linux distributions?
questions.explanation: Bash (Bourne Again Shell) is the default shell for most Linux distributions. It provides command-line interaction, scripting support, and features like command history, tab completion, and aliases.
questions.takeaway: Bash = Default Linux shell (Bourne Again Shell)

questions.question: Which command searches for a string pattern in files?
questions.explanation: grep (Global Regular Expression Print) searches for patterns in files or input. 'grep pattern file' prints matching lines. '-i' ignores case, '-r' searches recursively, '-v' inverts match.
questions.takeaway: grep = Search text patterns in files; -r recursive, -i case-insensitive

questions.question: Which command shows running processes in Linux?
questions.explanation: ps (process status) displays a snapshot of current processes. 'ps aux' shows all processes with details. 'ps -ef' shows all processes in full format. top/htop shows them in real-time.
questions.takeaway: ps = Process Status; ps aux shows all processes with CPU/memory

questions.question: Which directory contains device files in Linux?
questions.explanation: /dev contains device files that represent hardware devices and virtual devices. Examples: /dev/sda (first hard disk), /dev/null (null device), /dev/tty (terminal), /dev/zero (zero source).
questions.takeaway: /dev = Device files; /dev/sda=disk, /dev/null=null, /dev/tty=terminal

questions.question: What permission level does the number 4 represent in chmod?
questions.explanation: In Linux permissions: 4=Read (r), 2=Write (w), 1=Execute (x). These add up: 7=rwx, 6=rw-, 5=r-x, 4=r--. The three digits in chmod 754 represent Owner, Group, Others.
questions.takeaway: chmod: 4=Read, 2=Write, 1=Execute; 7=rwx, 6=rw-, 5=r-x, 4=r--

questions.question: Which command is used to change your current directory?
questions.explanation: cd (change directory) navigates to a different directory. 'cd ~' or 'cd' goes to home directory. 'cd ..' goes up one level. 'cd -' returns to previous directory.
questions.takeaway: cd = Change Directory; cd ~ = home, cd .. = up one level, cd - = previous dir

questions.question: What does the /var directory contain in Linux?
options: Variable data like logs and spools
questions.explanation: /var (variable) contains variable data that changes during system operation: log files (/var/log), mail spools (/var/mail), print spools (/var/spool), and temporary files that persist across reboots.
questions.takeaway: /var = Variable data (logs in /var/log, spools in /var/spool)

questions.question: Which command displays the hostname of a Linux system?
questions.explanation: hostname displays the current hostname of the system. 'hostname newname' temporarily changes it. Permanent changes require editing /etc/hostname. uname -n also shows hostname.
questions.takeaway: hostname = Display/set system hostname

questions.question: What does 'ls -a' do?
questions.explanation: 'ls -a' lists all files including hidden files (those starting with a dot like .bashrc, .ssh). Without -a, ls hides files starting with '.'. '-la' combines long format with hidden files.
questions.takeaway: ls -a = List ALL files including hidden (dot files)

questions.question: Which command compresses files using gzip in Linux?
questions.explanation: gzip compresses files using the DEFLATE algorithm. 'gzip file' creates file.gz and removes the original. 'gzip -d file.gz' or 'gunzip file.gz' decompresses it.
questions.takeaway: gzip = Compress file (creates .gz); gunzip = decompress .gz

questions.question: What does the /home directory contain?
questions.explanation: /home contains personal directories for regular users (e.g., /home/alice, /home/bob). Each user's home directory stores their personal files, configuration files, and shell history.
questions.takeaway: /home = User home directories (e.g., /home/username)

questions.question: Which command displays all environment variables in Linux?
questions.explanation: printenv displays all environment variables or a specific one: 'printenv PATH'. The 'env' command also lists all environment variables. 'echo $VAR' shows a specific variable's value.
questions.takeaway: printenv = Display environment variables; env also lists all

questions.question: Which directory contains the Linux kernel and boot files?
questions.explanation: /boot contains the Linux kernel (vmlinuz), initial ramdisk (initrd/initramfs), and bootloader configuration (grub). These files are essential for the system boot process.
questions.takeaway: /boot = Kernel, initrd, and GRUB bootloader files

questions.question: What does 'rm -f' do?
questions.explanation: 'rm -f' (force) removes files without prompting for confirmation, even if the file is write-protected. Combined with -r (rm -rf), it recursively removes directories without any prompts.
questions.takeaway: rm -f = Force delete without prompting; rm -rf = recursive force delete

questions.question: Which command links the current shell to the history of commands?
questions.explanation: history displays the command history list with line numbers. '!N' re-executes command number N. '!!' re-executes the last command. 'history -c' clears the history.
questions.takeaway: history = Show command history; !! = last command, !N = command #N

questions.question: Which command shows real-time process information with CPU and memory usage?
questions.explanation: top displays a real-time, dynamic view of running processes sorted by CPU usage. It shows CPU%, memory%, PID, and process names. Press 'q' to quit, 'k' to kill a process, 'M' to sort by memory.
questions.takeaway: top = Real-time process monitor; q=quit, k=kill, M=sort by memory

questions.question: What does 'ls -l' display?
options: Long listing with permissions, owner, size, and date
Only hidden files in long format
questions.explanation: 'ls -l' shows a long listing format: permissions (rwxrwxrwx), link count, owner, group, file size, modification date, and filename. 'ls -lh' adds human-readable sizes.
questions.takeaway: ls -l = Long listing (permissions, owner, size, date); ls -lh for human-readable sizes

questions.question: What is the purpose of the /proc filesystem in Linux?
options: Virtual filesystem exposing kernel and process information
questions.explanation: /proc is a virtual (pseudo) filesystem that exposes kernel and process information as files. /proc/cpuinfo shows CPU details, /proc/meminfo shows memory, /proc/[PID] shows per-process info. It is not stored on disk.
questions.takeaway: /proc = Virtual filesystem for kernel/process info; /proc/cpuinfo, /proc/meminfo

questions.question: What is a zombie process in Linux?
options: A process running in the background
A terminated process whose entry still exists in the process table
questions.explanation: A zombie process has completed execution but still has an entry in the process table because its parent has not read its exit status via wait(). It consumes no resources except a process table slot.
questions.takeaway: Zombie Process = Finished but not yet reaped by parent; exists in process table only

questions.question: What is an orphan process in Linux?
options: A child process whose parent has terminated
A process with no files open
questions.explanation: An orphan process is a child process whose parent has died. Linux automatically re-parents orphans to the init process (PID 1 / systemd), which then adopts and manages them.
questions.takeaway: Orphan Process = Parent died; adopted by init (PID 1 / systemd)

questions.question: Which command shows the open files of a running process?
questions.explanation: lsof (List Open Files) shows all files opened by processes. 'lsof -p PID' lists files for a specific process. 'lsof -i :80' shows what is using port 80. In Linux, everything is a file including sockets and pipes.
questions.takeaway: lsof = List Open Files; lsof -p PID, lsof -i :port to filter

questions.question: What does the sticky bit do when set on a directory?
options: Makes the directory executable by all
Only the file owner can delete their files within the directory
Prevents the directory from being deleted
questions.explanation: The sticky bit on a directory (like /tmp) ensures that only the file's owner, the directory's owner, or root can delete or rename files within it. Other users can still read and create files.
questions.takeaway: Sticky bit on dir = Only file owner/root can delete files within (used on /tmp)

questions.question: What does the setgid bit do on a directory?
options: Prevents group members from deleting files
New files inherit the directory's group
Sets the group to root for new files
questions.explanation: When setgid is set on a directory, new files and subdirectories created within inherit the directory's group ownership rather than the creator's primary group. Useful for shared project directories.
questions.takeaway: setgid on directory = New files inherit directory's group (shared projects)

questions.question: Which command shows network connections and listening ports in Linux?
questions.explanation: 'netstat -an' (or 'ss -an' in modern Linux) displays all network connections and listening ports with numeric addresses. '-t' shows TCP, '-u' shows UDP, '-l' shows only listening sockets.
questions.takeaway: netstat -an / ss -an = Show all network connections and listening ports

questions.question: What is the purpose of the /etc/fstab file?
options: Defines filesystems to mount at boot
questions.explanation: /etc/fstab (filesystem table) defines which filesystems are automatically mounted at boot, their mount points, types, and options. 'mount -a' mounts all entries in fstab.
questions.takeaway: /etc/fstab = Auto-mount table; defines what to mount where at boot

questions.question: What does the 'nice' command do in Linux?
questions.explanation: nice starts a process with a specified niceness value (-20 to 19). Higher niceness = lower priority. 'nice -n 10 cmd' starts cmd with low priority. 'renice' changes priority of a running process.
questions.takeaway: nice = Set process priority; higher nice value = lower priority (range: -20 to 19)

questions.question: Which command is used to schedule one-time tasks in Linux?
questions.explanation: The 'at' command is used to schedule one-time tasks at a specific future time in Linux. 'cron' handles recurring tasks; 'at' handles single-occurrence scheduling.
questions.takeaway: at = Schedule one-time tasks at specific future time; cron = Recurring tasks

questions.question: What is the purpose of the /etc/sudoers file?
options: Defines sudo privileges for users and groups
questions.explanation: /etc/sudoers controls which users/groups can run which commands with sudo. It must be edited with 'visudo' (which validates syntax). Format: 'user ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL' grants full sudo access.
questions.takeaway: /etc/sudoers = sudo privileges config; edit with visudo (never directly)

questions.question: What does 'nohup command &' do?
options: Runs command immune to hangup signals in background
questions.explanation: nohup (no hang-up) runs a command that ignores the SIGHUP signal, so it continues running after the user logs out. '&' puts it in the background. Output goes to nohup.out by default.
questions.takeaway: nohup cmd & = Run in background, continue after logout (immune to SIGHUP)

questions.question: What is the purpose of swap space in Linux?
options: Provides extra virtual memory when RAM is full
questions.explanation: Swap space extends virtual memory by using disk space when physical RAM is exhausted. When RAM fills up, the kernel moves inactive memory pages to swap. Accessing swapped memory is much slower than RAM.
questions.takeaway: Swap = Disk-based extension of RAM; used when physical memory is full (slow)

questions.question: Which command shows detailed CPU information in Linux?
questions.explanation: lscpu displays detailed CPU architecture information including number of CPUs, cores, threads, CPU model, cache sizes, and supported instruction sets. /proc/cpuinfo provides raw kernel-level CPU information.
questions.takeaway: lscpu = CPU architecture details; /proc/cpuinfo = raw CPU info

questions.question: What is the difference between a hard link and a symbolic link?
options: Hard links can span filesystems, symbolic links cannot
Hard links share the same inode; symbolic links point to the file path
Symbolic links share the same inode; hard links do not
questions.explanation: Hard links share the same inode number as the original file. Symbolic links are separate files containing a path reference. Hard links cannot span filesystems or link to directories.
questions.takeaway: Hard link = Same inode, same data; Symlink = Path reference, can cross filesystems

questions.question: What does 'awk "{print $2}" file' do?
options: Prints the second line of the file
Prints the second field of each line
Prints the second character of each line
questions.explanation: In awk, $1, $2, $3 refer to fields (columns) separated by whitespace. '{print $2}' prints the second field of each line. $0 refers to the entire line.
questions.takeaway: awk $N = Field N of each line; $0 = entire line, $1 = first field

questions.question: Which command shows the routing table in Linux?
questions.explanation: 'route -n' displays the kernel routing table with numeric IPs. The modern equivalent is 'ip route show'. The routing table determines where packets go based on destination network.
questions.takeaway: route -n = Show routing table; 'ip route show' is the modern command

questions.question: What is SELinux enforcing mode?
options: SELinux logs violations but does not block
SELinux logs and blocks policy violations
questions.explanation: In Enforcing mode, SELinux actively enforces its policies and blocks any access that violates them, logging the denials. Permissive mode only logs without blocking. Disabled turns SELinux off entirely.
questions.takeaway: SELinux Enforcing = Block + Log violations; Permissive = Log only; Disabled = Off

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'systemctl enable servicename'?
options: Checks if the service is running
Starts the service and enables auto-start at boot
Only enables the service to auto-start at boot
questions.explanation: 'systemctl enable' creates symlinks so the service starts automatically at boot. It does NOT start the service immediately. 'systemctl start' starts it now. 'systemctl enable --now' does both.
questions.takeaway: systemctl enable = Auto-start at boot (not immediately); --now does both

questions.question: Which log file records authentication events in Linux?
questions.explanation: /var/log/auth.log (Debian/Ubuntu) or /var/log/secure (RHEL/CentOS) records authentication events: successful/failed logins, sudo usage, SSH sessions, and su attempts. Essential for security auditing.
questions.takeaway: /var/log/auth.log (Ubuntu) or /var/log/secure (RHEL) = Auth/login events

questions.question: What does 'kill -9 PID' do?
questions.explanation: kill -9 sends SIGKILL (signal 9) to a process, forcing immediate termination. Unlike SIGTERM (15) which can be caught/ignored, SIGKILL cannot be intercepted. The kernel terminates the process immediately.
questions.takeaway: kill -9 = SIGKILL; cannot be caught or ignored; immediate forced termination

questions.question: What does 'chmod +x script.sh' do?
options: Adds execute permission for owner, group, and others
Adds execute permission for the owner only
questions.explanation: 'chmod +x' adds execute permission for all classes (owner, group, others). To add for owner only: 'chmod u+x'. Symbolic notation: u=user, g=group, o=others, a=all.
questions.takeaway: chmod +x = Add execute for all; u+x = owner only; g+x = group only

questions.question: What does 'strace' do in Linux?
options: Traces system calls made by a process
questions.explanation: strace traces system calls and signals made by a process, showing what the process is doing at the kernel level. 'strace -p PID' attaches to a running process. Invaluable for debugging application issues.
questions.takeaway: strace = Trace system calls of a process; strace -p PID attaches to running process

questions.question: Which command is used to add a user to a group in Linux?
questions.explanation: 'usermod -aG groupname username' adds a user to a supplementary group (-a = append, -G = supplementary group). Without -a, -G replaces all supplementary groups. Changes take effect at next login.
questions.takeaway: usermod -aG group user = Add user to supplementary group (changes after re-login)

questions.question: What does the 'source' command (or dot '.') do in bash?
options: Downloads and runs a script from the internet
Executes script in current shell environment
questions.explanation: 'source file' (or '. file') executes a script within the current shell's environment, so variable assignments and function definitions persist after the script completes. Used to reload ~/.bashrc.
questions.takeaway: source / . = Execute script in CURRENT shell; variable changes persist

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'rsync' command?
options: Efficiently synchronizes files and directories locally or remotely
Synchronizes system time with NTP servers
questions.explanation: rsync efficiently copies and synchronizes files, transferring only changed portions. 'rsync -avz src/ dest/' syncs with verbose output and compression. It works locally or over SSH for remote sync.
questions.takeaway: rsync = Efficient file sync; transfers only changes; works locally and over SSH

questions.question: What is a daemon in Linux?
options: A process waiting for user input
A background service process that runs continuously
questions.explanation: A daemon is a long-running background process that provides system services. Examples: sshd (SSH server), httpd/nginx (web server), crond (cron scheduler), systemd (init system). Daemons typically start at boot.
questions.takeaway: Daemon = Background service process (e.g., sshd, httpd, crond)

questions.question: What does 'ps aux | grep nginx' do?
questions.explanation: This pipes ps aux output (all processes) to grep which filters lines containing 'nginx', effectively showing only nginx-related processes. Piping (|) sends stdout of one command as stdin to the next.
questions.takeaway: ps aux | grep name = Find processes matching name using pipe

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'crontab -e' in Linux?
options: Edit the current user's cron job schedule
questions.explanation: 'crontab -e' opens the current user's crontab file for editing. Format: 'min hour day month weekday command'. 'crontab -l' lists existing jobs. 'crontab -r' removes all cron jobs for the user.
questions.takeaway: crontab -e = Edit user cron jobs; -l = list, -r = remove all

questions.question: What does 'free -h' display?
options: RAM and swap usage in human-readable format
Free file handles in the kernel
questions.explanation: 'free -h' shows physical RAM and swap usage in human-readable format (KB/MB/GB). Fields: total, used, free, shared, buffers/cache, available. The 'available' column shows usable memory for new processes.
questions.takeaway: free -h = Show RAM and swap usage; 'available' column is actually usable memory

questions.question: What is the purpose of SSH key-based authentication?
options: Passwordless authentication using public/private key pairs
questions.explanation: SSH key-based authentication uses asymmetric cryptography: the client has a private key, the server has the matching public key in ~/.ssh/authorized_keys. Login succeeds without a password by proving possession of the private key.
questions.takeaway: SSH keys = Passwordless auth; private key stays local, public key on server

questions.question: What is the /etc/crontab file used for?
options: System-wide cron jobs with user field
questions.explanation: /etc/crontab is the system-wide crontab that includes a username field specifying which user runs each job. Format: 'min hr day mon dow USER command'. User-specific crontabs via crontab -e do not have the user field.
questions.takeaway: /etc/crontab = System-wide cron with user field; crontab -e = per-user cron

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'journalctl -u servicename' in Linux?
options: Removes journal logs for a service
Updates the journal for a service
Restarts a service and journals the event
Shows systemd journal logs for a specific service
questions.explanation: 'journalctl -u servicename' queries systemd journal entries for a specific service. '-f' follows in real-time, '-n 50' shows last 50 lines, '--since today' filters by date. It replaces reading individual log files.
questions.takeaway: journalctl -u service = View systemd logs for service; -f follows live; --since filters

questions.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
options: Processes share memory; threads do not
Threads share the same address space within a process; processes have separate address spaces
questions.explanation: A process has its own independent address space, file descriptors, and resources. Threads within the same process share the address space, code, data, and open files, but have their own stack, registers, and program counter. Threads are lighter to create and faster to context-switch.
questions.takeaway: Thread = Lightweight; shares process address space. Process = Independent address space.

questions.question: What is copy-on-write (COW) in the context of fork()?
options: Parent and child always get separate memory immediately
Only file data is shared between parent and child
Parent and child share memory pages until one writes, then a copy is made
COW is only used for shared memory segments
questions.explanation: With Copy-on-Write (COW), after fork() the parent and child share the same physical memory pages marked read-only. When either process writes to a page, the kernel creates a separate copy for that process. This optimizes fork() for exec()-heavy workloads.
questions.takeaway: COW fork() = Shared memory until write then kernel copies only written pages

questions.question: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling?
options: Non-preemptive allows interrupts; preemptive does not
Preemptive OS can interrupt a running process; non-preemptive must wait for process to yield
Preemptive scheduling runs only one process at a time
questions.explanation: In preemptive scheduling, the OS can interrupt a running process (e.g., when a higher-priority process arrives or time quantum expires) and switch to another. In non-preemptive (cooperative) scheduling, the running process must voluntarily yield the CPU.
questions.takeaway: Preemptive = OS can interrupt process; Non-preemptive = Process yields voluntarily

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Linux OOM killer?
options: Kills processes using too much CPU
Terminates processes to free memory when system is critically low on RAM
questions.explanation: The OOM (Out Of Memory) killer is invoked by the kernel when the system runs out of memory. It selects and kills one or more processes based on oom_score to free memory and prevent a complete system freeze.
questions.takeaway: OOM Killer = Kernel kills processes when RAM+swap exhausted; uses oom_score

questions.question: What is a named pipe (FIFO) in Linux?
options: A pipe with a name stored in /etc
A special file that acts as an IPC channel between unrelated processes
A pipe with better performance than anonymous pipes
A pipe that works only between parent and child processes
questions.explanation: A named pipe (FIFO) is a special file in the filesystem that provides IPC between unrelated processes. Unlike anonymous pipes (|), FIFOs persist in the filesystem and can be used by any process with permission. Created with 'mkfifo'.
questions.takeaway: Named Pipe/FIFO = IPC for unrelated processes; persists in filesystem; mkfifo creates it

questions.question: What does 'ulimit -n' command control?
options: Maximum number of open file descriptors per process
questions.explanation: 'ulimit -n' sets/shows the maximum number of open file descriptors (files, sockets, pipes) a process can have simultaneously. The default is typically 1024. High-traffic servers (nginx, databases) need this increased.
questions.takeaway: ulimit -n = Max open file descriptors per process (default 1024; increase for servers)

questions.question: What does the kernel parameter 'vm.swappiness' control?
options: The kernel's tendency to swap out memory pages (0=avoid swap, 100=aggressive swap)
The size of the swap partition
How fast swap space is allocated
questions.explanation: vm.swappiness (0-100) controls how aggressively the kernel swaps memory pages. 0 means swap only to avoid OOM; 100 means swap aggressively. Default is 60. Database servers often use 1-10.
questions.takeaway: vm.swappiness = 0 (avoid swap) to 100 (aggressive); default 60; set low for DBs

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'sysctl' in Linux?
options: Views and modifies kernel parameters at runtime
questions.explanation: sysctl reads and modifies kernel parameters exposed via /proc/sys/. 'sysctl -a' lists all parameters. 'sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1' enables IP forwarding. Persistent changes go in /etc/sysctl.conf.
questions.takeaway: sysctl = View/modify kernel parameters; persist in /etc/sysctl.conf

questions.question: What is the purpose of Linux namespaces?
options: Isolating system resources so processes see different views of the system
questions.explanation: Linux namespaces isolate system resources between processes. Types: PID (process IDs), Network, Mount, UTS (hostname), IPC, User, Cgroup. Namespaces are the foundation of container technology (Docker, Kubernetes).
questions.takeaway: Namespaces = Resource isolation for processes; foundation of containers (Docker)

questions.question: What is a cgroup (control group) in Linux?
options: A kernel feature for limiting and isolating resource usage of process groups
A group of users with common permissions
A group of related kernel modules
questions.explanation: cgroups (control groups) limit and account for resources (CPU, memory, disk I/O, network) used by groups of processes. Docker and Kubernetes use cgroups to enforce container resource limits.
questions.takeaway: cgroups = Limit CPU/memory/disk per process group; used by Docker/K8s

questions.question: What is POSIX in the context of Linux?
options: A set of standards specifying the interface of Unix-like operating systems
questions.explanation: POSIX (Portable Operating System Interface) is a family of IEEE standards defining Unix-like OS APIs for portability. It defines system calls, library functions, shell utilities, and threading (pthreads). Linux is mostly POSIX-compliant.
questions.takeaway: POSIX = Unix portability standards (IEEE); Linux is mostly POSIX-compliant

questions.question: What is the role of the init process (PID 1) in Linux?
options: The first user-space process that starts all other processes and reaps orphans
The process that loads the kernel
The first kernel thread managing hardware
A security monitor for the boot process
questions.explanation: The init process (PID 1 — traditionally SysVinit, now typically systemd) is the first user-space process started by the kernel. It initializes the system, starts services, and reaps zombie processes.
questions.takeaway: PID 1 (init/systemd) = First user-space process; starts all services; reaps zombies

questions.question: What is the purpose of LVM snapshots?
options: Create point-in-time consistent copies of logical volumes for backup
questions.explanation: LVM snapshots create a point-in-time consistent copy of a logical volume using COW (Copy-on-Write). Only changed blocks are stored in the snapshot. Used for backups while the LV stays online.
questions.takeaway: LVM Snapshot = Point-in-time COW copy; enables live backup without downtime

questions.question: What does 'ip route add 192.168.2.0/24 via 10.0.0.1' do?
options: Adds a static route to 192.168.2.0/24 via gateway 10.0.0.1
questions.explanation: This command adds a static route to the 192.168.2.0/24 network through the gateway 10.0.0.1. The kernel routing table uses this to forward packets destined for that subnet through the specified gateway.
questions.takeaway: ip route add NETWORK via GATEWAY = Add static route to routing table

questions.question: What is the Linux kernel's role in memory overcommit?
options: Memory overcommit is a userspace feature
Linux can allocate more virtual memory than physical RAM+swap banking on not all being used at once
Linux limits memory allocation to physical RAM only
questions.explanation: Memory overcommitment allows the kernel to allocate more virtual memory than physically available (RAM+swap). Controlled by vm.overcommit_memory: 0=heuristic, 1=always allow, 2=never exceed. Risky as it triggers OOM killer if wrong.
questions.takeaway: Memory Overcommit = Allocate more virtual than physical; vm.overcommit_memory controls it

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Linux audit framework (auditd)?
options: Tracking and logging system events for security compliance and forensics
questions.explanation: auditd is the Linux audit daemon that records security-relevant system calls, file accesses, and user actions to /var/log/audit/audit.log. Required for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and other compliance frameworks.
questions.takeaway: auditd = Linux security audit daemon; logs syscalls/file access to /var/log/audit/

questions.question: What is the difference between hard and soft limits in ulimit?
options: Hard limits can be raised by any user; soft limits cannot
Hard limits apply to root; soft limits to users
Soft limit is the current enforced limit; hard limit is the ceiling a user can raise to
questions.explanation: ulimit soft limits are the currently enforced limits that processes cannot exceed. Hard limits are the maximum ceiling that unprivileged users can raise their soft limits up to. Only root can raise the hard limit.
questions.takeaway: Soft limit = Enforced limit; Hard limit = Ceiling users can raise soft limit to

questions.question: What is the purpose of TCP SYN cookies?
options: Protect against SYN flood DDoS attacks without maintaining state in the SYN backlog
questions.explanation: SYN cookies protect against SYN flood attacks where attackers send many SYN packets to exhaust the connection table. With SYN cookies, the server encodes connection info in the sequence number, not in memory.
questions.takeaway: SYN Cookies = SYN flood defense; encode state in sequence number, not memory

questions.question: What does the 'mmap()' system call do?
options: Maps files or devices into process memory address space
Migrates memory pages between NUMA nodes
questions.explanation: mmap() maps files or devices directly into a process's virtual address space. Reading/writing the mapped memory directly accesses the file. Used for fast file I/O, shared memory between processes, and loading executables.
questions.takeaway: mmap() = Map file/device into process address space; fast I/O, shared memory

questions.question: What is the Linux epoll mechanism used for?
options: Scalable I/O event notification for handling thousands of file descriptors efficiently
Extended kernel polling for hardware interrupts
questions.explanation: epoll is a Linux kernel mechanism for scalable I/O event notification. Unlike select() or poll() which scan all file descriptors, epoll is O(1) for event monitoring. Used by high-performance servers (nginx, Node.js).
questions.takeaway: epoll = O(1) scalable I/O event notification; used by nginx, Node.js for many connections

questions.question: What is the difference between 'grep' and 'egrep'?
options: egrep supports extended regular expressions like +, ?, |, () without backslash
grep can search multiple files; egrep cannot
egrep is faster due to indexing
questions.explanation: egrep (equivalent to grep -E) supports Extended Regular Expressions (ERE), allowing metacharacters like + (one or more), ? (zero or one), | (alternation), and () (grouping) without backslashes.
questions.takeaway: egrep = grep -E; supports +, ?, |, () without backslash (Extended RegEx)

questions.question: What does 'ldd /usr/bin/ls' show?
options: Lines delimiters and directories in ls output
The logical disk device for ls
The shared libraries required by the ls binary
questions.explanation: ldd (List Dynamic Dependencies) shows the shared libraries (.so files) required by a binary and where they resolve from. If a library shows 'not found', the program will fail to run.
questions.takeaway: ldd binary = List shared library dependencies; 'not found' = missing lib = program fails

questions.question: What is the purpose of /etc/ld.so.conf and ldconfig in Linux?
options: Define directories for shared library search; ldconfig updates the library cache
Configure the linker for static libraries only
questions.explanation: /etc/ld.so.conf lists directories containing shared libraries. 'ldconfig' reads this file, updates /etc/ld.so.cache (the shared library cache), and creates necessary symlinks. Run after installing new shared libraries.
questions.takeaway: /etc/ld.so.conf + ldconfig = Configure and cache shared library search paths

questions.question: What is the purpose of eBPF in Linux?
options: A Berkeley Packet Filter for only network filtering
A kernel technology allowing safe programs to run in kernel space for observability and security
questions.explanation: eBPF (extended Berkeley Packet Filter) allows programs to run safely in Linux kernel space without modifying kernel source or loading modules. It powers modern observability tools (bcc, bpftrace) and security enforcement (Cilium).
questions.takeaway: eBPF = Run safe programs in kernel space; powers tracing, networking, security tools

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'perf' tool in Linux?
options: A performance analysis tool using hardware and software performance counters
A tool to optimize filesystem performance
A performance benchmarking tool for web servers
questions.explanation: perf is Linux's built-in performance analysis tool that uses CPU hardware counters and kernel tracepoints. 'perf stat cmd' shows performance statistics. 'perf record/report' profiles CPU usage.
questions.takeaway: perf = Linux performance profiler using hardware counters; perf stat, perf record

questions.question: What is the purpose of initramfs (initial RAM filesystem)?
options: A RAM disk for swap space
A temporary root filesystem loaded into RAM during boot for mounting the real root filesystem
questions.explanation: initramfs (or initrd) is a temporary compressed root filesystem loaded into RAM during boot. It contains drivers and tools needed to mount the actual root filesystem. After mounting root, it is discarded.
questions.takeaway: initramfs = Temporary RAM-based root FS during boot; mounts real root FS then discarded

questions.question: What is the difference between /dev/null and /dev/zero?
options: /dev/null discards all writes and returns EOF on read; /dev/zero provides infinite zero bytes on read
questions.explanation: /dev/null discards all data written to it and returns EOF when read — used to suppress output. /dev/zero provides an infinite stream of null bytes (0x00) when read — used to initialize memory or create zero-filled files with dd.
questions.takeaway: /dev/null = Black hole (discard writes, EOF on read); /dev/zero = Infinite zero bytes

questions.question: What is a race condition and how is it prevented?
options: Concurrent access to shared data with order-dependent results; prevented by synchronization primitives
A disk I/O conflict; prevented by journaling
A network collision; prevented by CSMA/CD
A process competing for CPU; prevented by scheduling
questions.explanation: A race condition occurs when two or more processes access shared data concurrently and the result depends on execution order. Prevented using synchronization: mutexes, semaphores, monitors, atomic operations, or read-write locks.
questions.takeaway: Race Condition = Concurrent shared data access; prevented by mutex/semaphore/atomic ops

questions.question: What does 'ss -tlnp' show?
options: Listening TCP sockets with process name and port numbers numeric
questions.explanation: 'ss -tlnp' shows TCP (-t) listening (-l) sockets with numeric (-n) ports and the process (-p) using each socket. ss (socket statistics) is the modern replacement for netstat.
questions.takeaway: ss -tlnp = Show listening TCP ports with process names (modern netstat replacement)

questions.question: What is the Linux kernel's slab allocator used for?
options: Efficiently manages kernel object memory by caching frequently allocated objects
questions.explanation: The slab allocator manages kernel memory allocation efficiently by maintaining caches of commonly used kernel objects (inodes, PCBs, network buffers). Pre-allocated slabs avoid the overhead of repeated malloc/free.
questions.takeaway: Slab Allocator = Kernel memory cache for frequent objects; reduces allocation overhead

questions.question: What is the purpose of the fork() system call?
options: Creates a new process as a copy of the calling process
Creates a new thread within the same process
questions.explanation: fork() creates a new process (child) that is an exact copy of the calling process (parent), including memory, file descriptors, and environment. fork() returns 0 to the child and the child's PID to the parent.
questions.takeaway: fork() = Creates child process as copy of parent; returns 0 to child, PID to parent

questions.question: What does POSIX signal SIGTERM (15) do compared to SIGKILL (9)?
options: SIGTERM requests graceful shutdown (can be caught); SIGKILL forces immediate termination (cannot be caught)
SIGTERM is more forceful than SIGKILL
SIGKILL allows cleanup; SIGTERM does not
questions.explanation: SIGTERM (15) is the default termination signal that can be caught or ignored by processes, allowing graceful cleanup. SIGKILL (9) cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored — the kernel terminates the process immediately without cleanup.
questions.takeaway: SIGTERM = Graceful shutdown request (catchable); SIGKILL = Forced kill (cannot be caught)

questions.question: What is the default port for Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) in Windows?
questions.explanation: RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) uses TCP port 3389 by default. It is Microsoft's proprietary protocol for remote desktop sessions. Changing the default port is a security best practice to reduce automated scanning attacks.
questions.takeaway: RDP = Port 3389 (TCP); Microsoft's remote desktop protocol

questions.question: Which tool manages local user accounts and groups in Windows?
questions.explanation: Local Users and Groups (lusrmgr.msc) manages local user accounts and groups on Windows systems. On domain-joined computers, domain accounts are managed via Active Directory Users and Computers (ADUC) on the domain controller.
questions.takeaway: lusrmgr.msc = Local Users and Groups management; ADUC for domain accounts

questions.question: Which command clears the DNS client cache in Windows?
questions.explanation: 'ipconfig /flushdns' clears the DNS resolver cache on a Windows client, forcing fresh DNS lookups. Useful when DNS records have changed and old cached entries are causing connection issues.
questions.takeaway: ipconfig /flushdns = Clear DNS client cache to force fresh lookups

questions.question: What does 'ipconfig /all' show in Windows?
options: Full TCP/IP configuration including MAC address, DNS, DHCP details
questions.explanation: 'ipconfig /all' shows complete TCP/IP configuration: IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers, DHCP server, MAC address (Physical Address), and lease information for each adapter.
questions.takeaway: ipconfig /all = Full network config including MAC, DNS, DHCP, lease info

questions.question: What is the Windows Registry Editor command?
questions.explanation: regedit.exe is the Windows Registry Editor, a graphical tool for viewing and editing the Windows registry. Always create a backup (File > Export) before making changes.
questions.takeaway: regedit = Windows Registry Editor; export backup before editing

questions.question: What does 'sfc /scannow' do in Windows?
options: Checks and repairs corrupted system files using cached copies
questions.explanation: SFC (System File Checker) '/scannow' scans all protected Windows system files and replaces corrupted or missing files. Run as Administrator. Log stored in %windir%\Logs\CBS\CBS.log.
questions.takeaway: sfc /scannow = Scan and repair corrupted Windows system files (run as Admin)

questions.question: What is the Windows Security Event Log used for?
questions.explanation: The Security Event Log records auditing events: successful/failed logons (4624/4625), privilege use, policy changes, object access, and account management. Viewable in Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security.
questions.takeaway: Security Event Log = Records logins, logoffs, privilege use, policy changes

questions.question: Which command verifies network connectivity in Windows?
questions.explanation: ping sends ICMP Echo Request packets to a host to test connectivity. 'ping hostname' or 'ping IP' tests reachability. 'ping -t IP' pings continuously until stopped with Ctrl+C.
questions.takeaway: ping = Test network connectivity (ICMP Echo Request); -t = continuous ping

questions.question: What does 'net start' and 'net stop' do in Windows?
options: Start/stop Windows services from command prompt
questions.explanation: 'net start ServiceName' starts a Windows service from the command prompt. 'net stop ServiceName' stops it. 'net start' with no argument lists all running services. Requires administrative privileges.
questions.takeaway: net start/stop ServiceName = Start/stop Windows services from command prompt

questions.question: Which Windows command shows all open TCP/IP connections?
questions.explanation: 'netstat -an' displays all active TCP/IP connections and listening ports with numeric addresses. '-b' shows the process name, '-o' shows the PID. Windows equivalent of Linux's ss/netstat.
questions.takeaway: netstat -an = Show all connections and listening ports; -b adds process names

questions.question: What is the function of Windows Server Manager?
options: A central console for managing server roles, features, and server health
questions.explanation: Server Manager is the primary server management console in Windows Server. It allows adding/removing roles and features, viewing server health/events, and managing remote servers from a single interface.
questions.takeaway: Server Manager = Central console for roles, features, and server health management

questions.question: What is NTFS and why is it preferred over FAT32?
options: NTFS is only for removable drives
NTFS is older; FAT32 is more modern
FAT32 supports all NTFS features but is faster
NTFS supports larger files (>4GB), permissions, encryption, journaling, and compression
questions.explanation: NTFS (New Technology File System) is Windows' primary filesystem. Advantages over FAT32: file sizes >4GB, NTFS permissions, EFS encryption, journaling (crash recovery), and compression. FAT32 has a 4GB file size limit.
questions.takeaway: NTFS vs FAT32: NTFS supports >4GB files, permissions, encryption, journaling

questions.question: What does 'diskpart' do in Windows?
options: A command-line tool for disk partitioning, volume management, and disk formatting
questions.explanation: diskpart is a powerful command-line disk partitioning tool. It can create, delete, format partitions, extend/shrink volumes, assign drive letters, and convert disk types (MBR to GPT).
questions.takeaway: diskpart = Command-line disk management; create/delete/format/extend partitions

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'tasklist' in Windows?
options: Displays running processes with PID and memory usage
questions.explanation: 'tasklist' displays all running processes with their PID, session, and memory usage. 'taskkill /PID N /F' forcefully terminates a process by PID. Windows equivalent of Linux's 'ps aux'.
questions.takeaway: tasklist = Show running processes (like ps aux); taskkill /PID N /F to terminate

questions.question: What does 'gpupdate /force' do in Windows?
questions.explanation: 'gpupdate /force' immediately refreshes all Group Policy settings on a Windows computer without waiting for the normal 90-minute automatic refresh interval. 'gpresult /r' shows applied policies.
questions.takeaway: gpupdate /force = Immediately refresh Group Policy; gpresult /r shows applied policies

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Windows hosts file?
options: Maps hostnames to IP addresses before DNS is queried
Blocks malicious hosts at the firewall level
Lists all network hosts discovered by WINS
questions.explanation: The Windows hosts file (C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts) maps hostnames to IP addresses locally, checked before DNS queries. Used for local name resolution and testing.
questions.takeaway: Windows hosts file = C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts; checked before DNS

questions.question: What is Windows BitLocker used for?
options: Full-drive encryption to protect data if the drive is stolen
questions.explanation: BitLocker provides full-disk encryption for entire volumes, protecting data if the drive is stolen. It uses AES encryption and typically integrates with TPM (Trusted Platform Module) for key storage.
questions.takeaway: BitLocker = Full disk encryption; uses AES + TPM for key storage

questions.question: What does RAID 1 provide?
options: Both speed and redundancy using parity
Striping across multiple disks without redundancy
questions.explanation: RAID 1 (mirroring) writes identical data to two or more disks simultaneously. If one disk fails, data is intact on the mirror. It provides redundancy but no improvement for write speed.
questions.takeaway: RAID 1 = Mirroring; exact copy on 2+ disks; full redundancy, no storage gain

questions.question: What is the role of the PDC Emulator FSMO in Active Directory?
options: Handles password changes, account lockout, time sync, and acts as legacy primary DC
questions.explanation: The PDC Emulator FSMO role handles: password changes (replicates immediately to PDC), account lockout processing, the authoritative time source for the domain (W32tm), and backward compatibility for legacy clients.
questions.takeaway: PDC Emulator = Password changes, account lockout, time source (NTP) for domain

questions.question: Which protocol does Windows use for file sharing across a network?
questions.explanation: SMB (Server Message Block) / CIFS (Common Internet File System) is the protocol Windows uses for file and printer sharing. Linux can access Windows shares via Samba. SMB uses port 445.
questions.takeaway: SMB/CIFS = Windows file sharing protocol (port 445); Linux uses Samba

questions.question: What command shows system information including OS version in Windows?
questions.explanation: 'systeminfo' displays detailed system information: OS version, build, installation date, system type, physical memory, domain/workgroup, and hotfixes installed. 'winver' shows a GUI version dialog.
questions.takeaway: systeminfo = Detailed OS/hardware info; winver = GUI version dialog

questions.question: What does 'whoami /all' show in Windows?
options: All running processes as current user
Current user, groups, SID, and privileges
questions.explanation: 'whoami' displays the current username. 'whoami /all' shows the username, SID, group memberships, and privileges. It is the Windows equivalent of the Linux 'id' command.
questions.takeaway: whoami /all = Show current user, groups, SID, and privileges

questions.question: What is Windows Defender?
options: Built-in antivirus and anti-malware protection in Windows
questions.explanation: Windows Defender (now Windows Defender Antivirus) is Microsoft's built-in real-time antivirus and anti-malware protection. It is managed via Windows Security Center or Group Policy.
questions.takeaway: Windows Defender = Built-in antivirus/anti-malware (Windows Security)

questions.question: What is the purpose of Active Directory Sites and Services?
options: Managing AD replication topology between sites to optimize replication traffic
questions.explanation: Active Directory Sites and Services manages the physical topology of the AD network, defining sites (groups of well-connected subnets) and site links. It controls replication between DCs in different sites.
questions.takeaway: AD Sites and Services = Manage replication topology between physical locations/sites

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'net share' command in Windows?
questions.explanation: 'net share' lists all shared folders on the system. 'net share ShareName=C:\path' creates a share. 'net share ShareName /delete' removes it. Administrative shares (C$, ADMIN$, IPC$) are created automatically.
questions.takeaway: net share = List/create/remove shared folders; admin shares (C$, ADMIN$) are automatic

questions.question: What is the Windows Event Viewer used for?
options: A live dashboard for server metrics
Viewing and analyzing Windows event logs (System, Application, Security)
questions.explanation: Event Viewer displays Windows event logs: System (OS events), Application (app events), and Security (audit events like logon/logoff and policy changes). Accessible via eventvwr.msc.
questions.takeaway: Event Viewer = Windows log viewer; System, Application, Security logs (eventvwr.msc)

questions.question: What does the 'tracert' command do in Windows?
options: Shows the network path (hops) to a destination with latency
questions.explanation: tracert (trace route) shows the path packets take to reach a destination, displaying each router hop with its IP and round-trip latency. Linux equivalent is traceroute. Useful for diagnosing network routing issues.
questions.takeaway: tracert = Trace network path; shows each router hop and latency to destination

questions.question: What is Windows PowerShell used for?
options: Command-line shell and scripting language for Windows administration and automation
Only for scripting, not interactive use
questions.explanation: PowerShell is Microsoft's task automation framework combining an interactive command-line shell with a scripting language. It uses verb-noun cmdlets (e.g., Get-Process, Set-Service) and can manage the entire Windows ecosystem.
questions.takeaway: PowerShell = Windows admin shell + scripting; verb-noun cmdlets (Get-Service, etc.)

questions.question: What is the purpose of Windows Server DHCP role?
options: Automatically assigns IP configuration to network clients
questions.explanation: The DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) server role automatically assigns IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS server addresses to network clients, eliminating manual static IP configuration.
questions.takeaway: DHCP role = Auto-assign IP, subnet, gateway, DNS to clients

questions.question: What is the Windows Task Manager shortcut?
questions.explanation: Ctrl+Shift+Esc opens Task Manager directly. Ctrl+Alt+Del opens the Windows security screen where Task Manager is one option. Task Manager shows processes, performance, app history, startup, users, services, and details.
questions.takeaway: Ctrl+Shift+Esc = Direct Task Manager shortcut; shows processes, CPU/memory usage

questions.question: What is a Windows Service?
options: A long-running background application that starts automatically and runs without user interaction
An application installed from the Microsoft Store
questions.explanation: A Windows Service is a long-running background application managed by the Service Control Manager (SCM). Services can be configured to start automatically at boot, run under specific accounts, and restart on failure.
questions.takeaway: Windows Service = Background app managed by SCM; can auto-start at boot

questions.question: What is the purpose of Windows Update Services (WSUS)?
options: Centrally manages and deploys Microsoft updates across the organization
questions.explanation: WSUS (Windows Server Update Services) allows IT administrators to manage and distribute Microsoft product updates centrally. Clients download updates from WSUS instead of Microsoft's servers, saving bandwidth.
questions.takeaway: WSUS = Centralized Microsoft update management; clients pull from WSUS, not Microsoft

questions.question: What does the 'net user' command do in Windows?
options: Shows users logged into the system
Manages local user accounts (create, delete, view)
questions.explanation: 'net user' manages local user accounts. 'net user username password /add' creates a user. 'net user username /delete' removes a user. 'net user username' shows account details.
questions.takeaway: net user = Manage local users; /add creates, /delete removes, no switch=view info

questions.question: What is the difference between Domain Local, Global, and Universal groups in Active Directory?
options: Global groups cannot be nested; Domain Local can
Domain Local = resource access in one domain; Global = users in one domain; Universal = across forest
Universal groups replicate less than Global groups
They are different names for the same type of group
questions.explanation: Domain Local groups assign permissions to resources in their domain (can contain any user/group). Global groups organize users within one domain. Universal groups work across the forest and are stored in Global Catalog.
questions.takeaway: AD Groups: DL=resource access (any member), Global=one-domain users, Universal=forest-wide

questions.question: What is Windows PowerShell Remoting and which ports does it use?
options: PowerShell scripts running remotely on port 22
Remote PowerShell sessions using WS-Management on port 5985 (HTTP) / 5986 (HTTPS)
Remote PowerShell using DCOM on port 135
questions.explanation: PowerShell Remoting uses WS-Management (WSMan) protocol. HTTP uses port 5985; HTTPS uses port 5986. 'Enable-PSRemoting' enables it. 'Enter-PSSession -ComputerName host' opens an interactive remote session.
questions.takeaway: PS Remoting = WinRM/WSMan; HTTP:5985, HTTPS:5986; Enable-PSRemoting to activate

questions.question: What is the difference between Authentication and Authorization in Windows security?
options: Authentication is for local accounts only
They mean the same thing in Windows context
Authentication verifies identity; Authorization determines what you can access
questions.explanation: Authentication verifies the identity of a user (e.g., Kerberos ticket, NTLM challenge-response). Authorization determines what resources/permissions an authenticated user can access based on ACLs and group memberships.
questions.takeaway: Authentication = Who are you? (identity); Authorization = What can you do? (permissions)

questions.question: What is a Windows Access Control List (ACL)?
options: A list of access control entries (ACEs) defining what principals have what permissions on an object
The list of users who last accessed a resource
questions.explanation: An ACL (Access Control List) consists of ACEs (Access Control Entries). Each ACE specifies a principal (user/group), access mask (permissions), and allow/deny. DACL controls access; SACL controls auditing.
questions.takeaway: ACL = List of ACEs; DACL controls access, SACL controls auditing; in file Properties->Security

questions.question: What is the purpose of Windows Server IIS (Internet Information Services)?
options: A web server platform hosting HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, and SMTP services
questions.explanation: IIS (Internet Information Services) is Microsoft's web server platform for hosting web applications, websites, REST APIs, and FTP servers. It integrates with ASP.NET, PHP, and supports SSL/TLS for HTTPS.
questions.takeaway: IIS = Microsoft web server; hosts HTTP/HTTPS/FTP; integrates with ASP.NET

questions.question: What is the purpose of the SYSVOL share in Active Directory?
options: Stores Group Policy templates and logon/logoff scripts, replicated to all DCs
questions.explanation: SYSVOL is a shared folder replicated to all domain controllers using DFSR (DFS Replication). It contains Group Policy Object templates and logon/logoff scripts. Accessible as \\domain\SYSVOL.
questions.takeaway: SYSVOL = Shared folder on all DCs; stores GPO templates + scripts; replicated via DFSR

questions.question: What is Kerberos constrained delegation in Active Directory?
options: Allows a service to impersonate a user but only for access to specific target services
Limits Kerberos to specific network segments
Limits which accounts can use Kerberos authentication
questions.explanation: Constrained Delegation allows a service account to impersonate a user, but only to access pre-defined target services (not any service). It solves the 'double-hop' problem in multi-tier applications.
questions.takeaway: Kerberos Constrained Delegation = Impersonate user for specific services only (double-hop fix)

questions.question: What is Windows LAPS (Local Administrator Password Solution)?
options: A policy to lock out local admin accounts
Automatically manages and rotates the local administrator password on domain-joined computers
A password manager for domain admins
questions.explanation: LAPS automatically generates and rotates the local Administrator account password on domain-joined computers, storing it encrypted in Active Directory. This prevents pass-the-hash attacks by ensuring every machine has a unique, rotated local admin password.
questions.takeaway: LAPS = Auto-rotate local admin passwords; stored encrypted in AD; prevents pass-the-hash

questions.question: What is Windows Server DFS (Distributed File System)?
options: Distributes files across multiple cloud services
Provides a unified namespace and replication of shared folders across multiple servers
A distributed file system for Linux interop
questions.explanation: DFS has two components: DFS Namespaces (single logical path to multiple shared folders on different servers) and DFS Replication (synchronizes folder content between servers using DFSR).
questions.takeaway: DFS = Namespace (virtual path to multiple shares) + Replication (folder sync between servers)

questions.question: What is Windows Server Hyper-V?
options: A hypervisor for running virtual machines on Windows Server
questions.explanation: Hyper-V is Microsoft's Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisor that enables running multiple virtual machines on a single physical Windows Server host. It provides isolation, resource allocation, snapshots, live migration, and VM replication.
questions.takeaway: Hyper-V = Microsoft Type 1 hypervisor; runs VMs on Windows Server

questions.question: What is Windows Server Failover Clustering used for?
options: Providing high availability by automatically failing over to standby nodes
questions.explanation: Windows Server Failover Clustering (WSFC) groups servers (nodes) so if one fails, another automatically takes over its workloads (SQL Server, Hyper-V VMs, file servers). It uses shared storage or Storage Spaces Direct.
questions.takeaway: WSFC = High availability; workloads auto-failover to surviving node on failure

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'netsh' command in Windows?
options: A command-line tool for configuring network settings including firewall, interfaces, and protocols
questions.explanation: netsh (Network Shell) is a versatile command-line tool for configuring and displaying network settings: interface configuration, firewall rules, WLAN settings, TCP/IP parameters, and more.
questions.takeaway: netsh = Network configuration CLI; interfaces, firewall, WLAN, TCP/IP settings

questions.question: What is the purpose of Windows Server ADCS (Active Directory Certificate Services)?
options: Certifies Active Directory users for training
Issues and manages digital certificates (PKI) for users, computers, and services
questions.explanation: ADCS implements a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) for the organization, issuing X.509 digital certificates for authentication, encryption (EFS, BitLocker), email signing, SSL/TLS, and code signing.
questions.takeaway: ADCS = Windows PKI; issues certificates for auth, SSL, EFS, code signing

questions.question: What does 'dism /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth' do?
options: Repairs Windows system image using Windows Update as source
questions.explanation: DISM '/RestoreHealth' scans for corrupted Windows image files and repairs them using Windows Update as the source. Run this before or after sfc /scannow for comprehensive system file repair.
questions.takeaway: dism /RestoreHealth = Repair Windows image from Windows Update; use before/after sfc

questions.question: What is the NTLM authentication protocol?
options: A challenge-response authentication protocol used as a fallback when Kerberos is unavailable
questions.explanation: NTLM (NT LAN Manager) is a challenge-response authentication protocol. Kerberos is preferred in AD environments, but NTLM is used when Kerberos fails (workgroup connections, IP-based connections, older systems). Vulnerable to pass-the-hash attacks.
questions.takeaway: NTLM = Challenge-response auth; fallback when Kerberos unavailable; vulnerable to pass-the-hash

questions.question: What is a Windows SID (Security Identifier)?
options: A session ID assigned at login
A unique identifier assigned to each security principal (user, group, computer)
questions.explanation: A SID (Security Identifier) is a unique, immutable identifier for every security principal in Windows. ACLs store SIDs, not usernames, so deleting and recreating a user does not restore their access.
questions.takeaway: SID = Unique immutable ID for users/groups; ACLs use SIDs not usernames

questions.question: What is the difference between GPO Enforced and Block Inheritance in Group Policy?
options: Enforced prevents GPO from being blocked by child OUs; Block Inheritance prevents parent GPOs from applying
They are opposite settings with identical effect
Block Inheritance is stronger than Enforced
Enforced applies only to computer settings
questions.explanation: Block Inheritance on an OU prevents parent GPOs from applying to it. Enforced (No Override) on a GPO prevents it from being blocked by Block Inheritance on child OUs. Enforced always wins over Block Inheritance.
questions.takeaway: Block Inheritance = Block parent GPOs; Enforced = Cannot be blocked (wins over Block Inheritance)

questions.question: What is Windows Server Network Load Balancing (NLB)?
options: Distributes TCP/IP traffic across multiple servers for scalability and availability
questions.explanation: NLB (Network Load Balancing) distributes incoming TCP/IP traffic across multiple server nodes in a cluster. Used for web servers, terminal services, and VPN servers. All nodes share a virtual IP.
questions.takeaway: NLB = Distribute TCP/IP traffic across server cluster; shared virtual IP for scalability

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'robocopy' in Windows?
options: A robust file copy tool with advanced options including mirroring, logging, and retry
A backup tool for the registry
A remote copy tool for cloud storage
questions.explanation: robocopy (Robust File Copy) is an advanced file/directory copy tool. 'robocopy src dst /MIR' mirrors directories. It supports multi-threaded copying (/MT:N), retry on failure, logging, and copying only changed files.
questions.takeaway: robocopy = Advanced file copy; /MIR = mirror, /MT:N = multithreaded, retries on failure

questions.question: What does 'wevtutil' do in Windows?
options: A command-line tool for managing Windows Event Logs (export, clear, query)
Web event utility for IIS logging
Manages Windows event subscriptions for SIEM
questions.explanation: wevtutil (Windows Event Utility) manages Windows Event Logs from the command line. 'wevtutil qe System' queries the System log. 'wevtutil epl Security C:\backup.evtx' exports the Security log.
questions.takeaway: wevtutil = CLI Event Log management; qe=query, epl=export, cl=clear

questions.question: What is the purpose of a DNS Forwarder in Windows Server?
options: Sends DNS queries for external domains to another DNS server instead of querying root servers
Forwards all DNS requests to Active Directory
Forwards DNS zone transfers to secondary servers
Forwards DNS requests from clients to the DNS server
questions.explanation: A DNS Forwarder configures the DNS server to send queries it cannot resolve locally to another DNS server (like ISP DNS or 8.8.8.8). Conditional Forwarders forward specific domain queries to specific servers.
questions.takeaway: DNS Forwarder = Send unresolved queries to another DNS server; Conditional Forwarder = per-domain

questions.question: What is Windows Credential Guard?
options: A password manager for Active Directory
Uses virtualization-based security to protect credential hashes from theft
questions.explanation: Credential Guard uses Virtualization-Based Security (VBS) to isolate and protect NTLM/Kerberos credential hashes in a secure virtual environment. It prevents credential theft tools (like Mimikatz) from extracting hashes from LSASS memory.
questions.takeaway: Credential Guard = VBS protects NTLM/Kerberos hashes; blocks Mimikatz-style attacks

questions.question: What does 'net accounts' command do in Windows?
options: Displays or modifies password policy and lockout settings for the local machine
questions.explanation: 'net accounts' displays the local password policy settings: minimum password age, maximum age, minimum length, lockout threshold, and lockout duration. 'net accounts /maxpwage:90' sets max password age.
questions.takeaway: net accounts = View/modify local password and lockout policy settings

questions.question: What is Windows Server Desired State Configuration (DSC)?
options: A Windows Update desired configuration tool
A PowerShell-based configuration management platform ensuring servers stay in a defined state
questions.explanation: DSC (Desired State Configuration) is a PowerShell management platform that defines and enforces the desired configuration state of servers. DSC configurations are idempotent — applying them repeatedly results in the same desired state.
questions.takeaway: DSC = PowerShell config management; declares desired state; idempotent (like Ansible)

questions.question: What is Volume Shadow Copy Service (VSS) in Windows?
options: A framework for creating consistent point-in-time snapshots of volumes
A shadow drive letter for backup volumes
questions.explanation: VSS (Volume Shadow Copy Service) creates consistent point-in-time snapshots of volumes even while files are in use. Used by Windows Backup, SQL Server backup, and third-party backup tools.
questions.takeaway: VSS = Creates consistent volume snapshots while files are in use; used by backup tools

questions.question: What is Windows Resource Monitor (resmon.exe)?
options: Shows real-time CPU, memory, disk, and network usage per process
questions.explanation: Resource Monitor (resmon.exe) shows detailed real-time resource usage: CPU usage per process, memory working set, disk I/O per file, and network connections per process. More detailed than Task Manager for troubleshooting.
questions.takeaway: resmon.exe = Detailed real-time per-process CPU/memory/disk/network monitoring

questions.question: What is Windows Server Storage Spaces?
options: Software-defined storage that pools physical disks into virtual disks with redundancy
questions.explanation: Storage Spaces pools physical drives (HDD, SSD) into storage pools, from which virtual disks (spaces) with redundancy (mirroring, parity) can be created. Storage Spaces Direct (S2D) extends this to clustered environments.
questions.takeaway: Storage Spaces = Pool physical disks into virtual disks with mirroring/parity

questions.question: What is the Windows Protected Users security group?
options: A group that protects user accounts from deletion
A group with read-only AD access
A group for users with elevated privileges
A security group that prevents members from using weaker authentication like NTLM and RC4
questions.explanation: Protected Users group members cannot authenticate with NTLM, DES/RC4 Kerberos, unconstrained delegation, or have long-lived Kerberos tickets. It forces the use of stronger Kerberos AES encryption.
questions.takeaway: Protected Users group = Forces AES Kerberos; blocks NTLM, RC4, and unconstrained delegation

questions.question: What is Active Directory Recycle Bin?
options: A Recycle Bin for Group Policy Objects
Recycles unused SIDs for new accounts
Allows restoring deleted AD objects with all attributes intact without authoritative restore
questions.explanation: AD Recycle Bin (enabled in forest functional level 2008 R2+) allows restoring deleted AD objects (users, groups, OUs) with all their attributes intact for a configurable period (default 180 days).
questions.takeaway: AD Recycle Bin = Restore deleted objects with full attributes; no authoritative restore needed

questions.question: What is the purpose of Windows Event Forwarding (WEF)?
options: Monitoring system events in real-time locally
Collecting event logs from multiple remote computers to a central collector
questions.explanation: Windows Event Forwarding (WEF) collects event log entries from multiple remote computers to a central Windows Event Collector. Useful for centralized log management and SIEM integration without third-party agents.
questions.takeaway: WEF = Windows Event Forwarding; centralizes logs from multiple machines to collector

questions.question: What is the difference between RAID 5 and RAID 6?
options: RAID 5 tolerates 1 disk failure; RAID 6 tolerates 2 simultaneous disk failures using double parity
They use different stripe sizes but identical redundancy
RAID 5 uses double parity; RAID 6 uses single parity
RAID 6 is faster but less redundant than RAID 5
questions.explanation: RAID 5 uses single distributed parity and can survive 1 disk failure (requires minimum 3 disks). RAID 6 uses double distributed parity and survives 2 simultaneous disk failures (requires minimum 4 disks).
questions.takeaway: RAID 5 = 1 parity, 1 disk failure tolerance; RAID 6 = 2 parity, 2 disk failure tolerance

questions.question: What is the purpose of Windows Server ADFS (Active Directory Federation Services)?
options: Provides SSO and identity federation using SAML and WS-Federation protocols
questions.explanation: ADFS provides Single Sign-On (SSO) and identity federation between organizations or to cloud services using SAML 2.0 and WS-Federation. It allows users to authenticate once and access resources across trusted organizations.
questions.takeaway: ADFS = On-prem SSO federation using SAML/WS-Fed; enables cross-org/cloud SSO

questions.question: What is a Windows Group Managed Service Account (gMSA)?
options: A service account for managing groups
An auto-managed service account with automatic password rotation usable by multiple servers
questions.explanation: gMSA (Group Managed Service Account) is an AD-managed service account with automatic password rotation (managed by DCs) that can be used across multiple servers simultaneously. Eliminates manual service account password management.
questions.takeaway: gMSA = Auto-rotating service account; usable by multiple servers; no manual password management

questions.question: What is Kerberos pre-authentication and why is it important?
