options: A mechanism requiring clients to prove identity before the KDC issues a TGT, preventing offline brute-force attacks
A secondary authentication factor for Kerberos
questions.explanation: Kerberos pre-authentication requires the client to encrypt a timestamp with their password hash before the KDC issues a TGT. Without pre-auth (DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH flag), attackers can request a TGT for any account and brute-force it offline (ASREPRoasting attack).
questions.takeaway: Kerberos pre-auth = Prevents ASREPRoasting; clients prove identity before getting TGT

questions.question: What is a Pass-the-Hash (PtH) attack?
options: An attack that captures and replays password hashes to authenticate without knowing the plaintext
An attack that hashes all user passwords on a DC
Stealing hashed passwords by dictionary attack
questions.explanation: Pass-the-Hash uses a stolen NTLM password hash to authenticate as the user without knowing the plaintext password. Tools like Mimikatz extract hashes from LSASS memory. Mitigations: Credential Guard, Protected Users group, LAPS.
questions.takeaway: Pass-the-Hash = Use stolen NTLM hash to authenticate; mitigated by Credential Guard + LAPS

questions.question: What is a Golden Ticket attack in Active Directory?
options: An attack stealing the administrator's credentials
Forging a Kerberos TGT using the KRBTGT account hash, granting unrestricted domain access
An attack targeting Active Directory backup files
A social engineering attack targeting gold-level AD accounts
questions.explanation: A Golden Ticket is a forged Kerberos TGT created using the KRBTGT account's NTLM hash. Since it's signed by KRBTGT, the KDC trusts it and issues any requested service ticket. Requires resetting KRBTGT password twice to mitigate.
questions.takeaway: Golden Ticket = Forged TGT using KRBTGT hash; grants unlimited domain access

questions.question: What is the Windows SAM (Security Account Manager) database?
options: A database of all AD user accounts
A local database storing NTLM hashes for local user accounts on non-DC Windows machines
questions.explanation: The SAM (Security Account Manager) database stores NTLM password hashes for local user accounts on Windows workstations and member servers. On domain controllers, NTDS.dit stores domain account hashes instead.
questions.takeaway: SAM = Local account NTLM hash database on non-DC Windows; NTDS.dit on domain controllers

questions.question: What is Kerberoasting and how does it work?
options: Requesting Kerberos service tickets for SPNs and offline brute-forcing the service account password hash
Attacking Kerberos TGT tickets with brute force
Forging Kerberos tickets using the KDC secret
questions.explanation: Kerberoasting requests Kerberos TGS (service) tickets for accounts with SPNs (Service Principal Names). The ticket is encrypted with the service account's NTLM hash. Attackers take the ticket offline and brute-force it. Mitigate with long passwords or gMSA.
questions.takeaway: Kerberoasting = Offline crack SPN service ticket; mitigate with long passwords or gMSA

questions.question: What is a DCSync attack?
options: Syncing rogue domain controllers to steal data
A DDoS attack against domain controllers
A timing attack against DC authentication
An attack using AD replication APIs to extract password hashes from a DC without code execution on it
questions.explanation: DCSync abuses Active Directory replication rights (DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All) to request credential data from a DC as if the attacker were a replication partner. Used with Mimikatz to extract all domain password hashes.
questions.takeaway: DCSync = Abuse AD replication rights to dump all hashes; requires DS-Replication rights

questions.question: What is Windows Just Enough Administration (JEA)?
options: Limits Active Directory to essential features only
Provides role-based administrative access via constrained PowerShell endpoints with least privilege
Minimizes admin UI to only essential tasks
questions.explanation: JEA (Just Enough Administration) creates constrained PowerShell remoting endpoints where users can run only specific commands defined in role capabilities files, implementing least-privilege administration.
questions.takeaway: JEA = Constrained PS remoting; users get only the cmdlets they need (least privilege)

questions.question: What is Windows Virtualization-Based Security (VBS)?
options: Uses Hyper-V hypervisor to create an isolated memory region protecting sensitive OS components
A virtual private network security framework
Security for virtual machine guests only
questions.explanation: VBS uses Hyper-V to create an isolated Virtual Secure Mode (VSM) environment separate from the main OS. Features like Credential Guard, Device Guard, and HVCI run in this isolated environment, protected even from kernel-level malware.
questions.takeaway: VBS = Hyper-V-based isolation for Credential Guard, Device Guard, HVCI protection

questions.question: What is the purpose of the RID Master FSMO role?
options: Allocates blocks of Relative IDs (RIDs) to domain controllers for SID creation
Manages resource record IDs in DNS
questions.explanation: The RID (Relative ID) Master allocates pools of RIDs to each domain controller. When a DC creates a new object, it uses one RID from its pool appended to the domain SID to create a unique object SID.
questions.takeaway: RID Master = Allocates RID pools to DCs; each new object SID = Domain SID + unique RID

questions.question: What is the NTDS.dit file in Active Directory?
options: The Active Directory database file stored on all domain controllers containing all AD objects and password hashes
A Windows NT legacy directory file
questions.explanation: NTDS.dit is the primary AD database file at %SystemRoot%\NTDS\ntds.dit on domain controllers. It contains all AD objects (users, groups, computers, policies) and their attributes including password hashes. Extracting it enables offline credential dumping.
questions.takeaway: NTDS.dit = AD database on DCs; contains all objects + hashed passwords; high-value target

questions.question: What is a Silver Ticket attack in Active Directory?
options: A forged Kerberos service ticket using the service account hash, bypassing the KDC for lateral movement
A weaker version of a Golden Ticket using user credentials
A ticket used for cross-domain lateral movement
An attack targeting service account passwords
questions.explanation: A Silver Ticket is a forged Kerberos TGS (service ticket) signed with the target service account's NTLM hash (not KRBTGT). It does not require KDC communication and is stealthier than Golden Tickets.
questions.takeaway: Silver Ticket = Forged service ticket using service account hash; bypasses KDC; stealthy

questions.question: What is Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) and how does it differ from AppLocker?
options: AppLocker is more secure than WDAC
WDAC enforces code integrity at the kernel level via VBS; AppLocker enforces at user mode and can be bypassed
questions.explanation: WDAC enforces application whitelisting at the kernel level using VBS/HVCI, making it tamper-resistant even from admin-level attacks. AppLocker enforces at user-mode and can be bypassed by users with local admin rights.
questions.takeaway: WDAC = Kernel-level code integrity (VBS/HVCI); AppLocker = User-mode, bypassable by admins

questions.question: What is the impact of disabling SMB version 1 (SMBv1)?
options: Improves speed but reduces compatibility with SMBv2
Required for NTLM authentication to work
Removes support for EternalBlue/WannaCry exploit vector but may break legacy devices requiring SMBv1
questions.explanation: SMBv1 is the protocol exploited by EternalBlue (MS17-010), enabling WannaCry and NotPetya ransomware. Disabling SMBv1 removes this attack surface but may break legacy devices (old printers, NAS, XP clients).
questions.takeaway: SMBv1 = EternalBlue/WannaCry exploit vector; disable it unless legacy devices require it

questions.question: What is the Active Directory Tombstone Lifetime and why does it matter?
options: The time deleted AD objects are retained in tombstone state for replication propagation before permanent deletion
The time a DC remains in maintenance mode
The time a DC can be offline before it needs to be rebuilt
questions.explanation: When AD objects are deleted, they become tombstones retained for 180 days by default. If a DC is offline longer than the tombstone lifetime, it may have stale objects, requiring forced demotion and re-promotion.
questions.takeaway: Tombstone Lifetime = 180 days; DC offline longer than this = USN rollback risk

questions.question: What is the forest functional level and what does it control?
options: The minimum Windows Server OS version for DCs in the forest, enabling forest-wide AD features
The number of domains in a forest
The replication compression level across sites
questions.explanation: Forest Functional Level (FFL) sets the minimum Windows Server version that DCs in the entire forest must run. Raising FFL enables forest-wide features like AD Recycle Bin (2008 R2+) and privileged access management (2016+).
questions.takeaway: Forest Functional Level = Minimum DC OS version for entire forest; enables forest-wide features

questions.question: What is Secure Boot and how does it work?
options: Uses UEFI firmware to verify bootloader and OS signature preventing rootkits from loading before the OS
A secure multi-boot configuration management tool
Encrypts the boot partition with BitLocker
Boots Windows in safe mode by default
questions.explanation: Secure Boot uses UEFI firmware's database of trusted signatures to verify the bootloader and OS kernel before loading. Any unsigned or tampered bootloader/kernel is rejected, preventing bootkit/rootkit attacks.
questions.takeaway: Secure Boot = UEFI verifies bootloader signature; prevents bootkits loading before OS

questions.question: What is a Pass-the-Ticket attack in Windows?
options: Reusing expired Kerberos tickets after extending their lifetime
Stealing and reusing a valid Kerberos ticket from memory to authenticate as that user
Passing authentication tickets between domains illegally
questions.explanation: Pass-the-Ticket uses a stolen valid Kerberos ticket (TGT or service ticket) extracted from LSASS memory (e.g., via Mimikatz) to impersonate a user without needing their password. Mitigated by short ticket lifetimes and Credential Guard.
questions.takeaway: Pass-the-Ticket = Steal valid Kerberos ticket from memory then authenticate as victim

questions.question: What is Active Directory multi-master replication?
options: AD replicates only from read-write DCs to RODCs
AD uses single-master replication from PDC to all DCs
All writable DCs accept changes independently and replicate to all others using USN tracking
questions.explanation: AD uses multi-master replication — all writable DCs accept changes independently. Changes replicate between DCs using Update Sequence Numbers (USN) and High Watermark Vectors. DFSR replicates SYSVOL. Within-site replication is immediate; cross-site uses site link schedules.
questions.takeaway: AD = Multi-master replication; all DCs accept changes; USN tracks what has been replicated

questions.question: What is Privileged Access Workstation (PAW)?
options: A workstation with elevated hardware specs
A dedicated, hardened workstation used exclusively for privileged administrative tasks
A VM-based workstation for privileged accounts
A portable admin laptop for field technicians
questions.explanation: A PAW is a dedicated, hardened computer used exclusively for privileged tasks (managing DCs, AD, Tier 0 systems). It is internet-isolated, malware-resistant, and never used for email or web browsing.
questions.takeaway: PAW = Dedicated hardened admin workstation; internet-isolated; for privileged tasks only

questions.question: What is Windows Token Impersonation in privilege escalation?
options: Using OAuth tokens for Windows authentication
Replacing system tokens with forged ones
Duplicating access tokens of higher-privileged processes to gain elevated privileges
questions.explanation: Windows access tokens represent the security context of a process/thread. A process with SeImpersonatePrivilege can duplicate another process's token to run code as that user. Used in Potato exploits to escalate from NETWORK SERVICE to SYSTEM.
questions.takeaway: Token Impersonation = Duplicate high-priv token for PE; Potato exploits abuse SeImpersonatePrivilege

questions.question: What is Network Policy Server (NPS) in Windows Server?
options: Implements RADIUS for centralized authentication, authorization, and accounting of VPN/wireless/802.1X clients
questions.explanation: NPS acts as a RADIUS server for authenticating and authorizing network access requests from VPN, wireless access points, and 802.1X switches. It validates credentials against AD and applies network policies.
questions.takeaway: NPS = Windows RADIUS; authenticates VPN/wireless/802.1X via AD; logs accounting

questions.question: What is Windows Event ID 4624?
options: Successful account logon event with logon type and authentication details
questions.explanation: Event ID 4624 (Security log) indicates a successful logon. Key fields: Logon Type (2=Interactive, 3=Network, 10=RemoteInteractive/RDP, 5=Service), Account Name, Workstation, and Authentication Package.
questions.takeaway: Event 4624 = Successful logon; Logon Type 2=interactive, 3=network, 10=RDP, 5=service

questions.question: What is LDAP injection and how does it affect Active Directory?
options: A network injection attack on LDAP traffic
An LDAP protocol version downgrade attack
Attackers inject malicious LDAP filter syntax via unsanitized input to bypass authentication or extract AD data
A buffer overflow in the LDAP service
questions.explanation: LDAP injection occurs when user-supplied input is unsanitized and directly embedded in LDAP queries. Attackers can modify filter logic to bypass authentication or extract unauthorized data from Active Directory.
questions.takeaway: LDAP Injection = Malicious LDAP filter via unsanitized input bypass auth or dump AD data

questions.question: What is the Windows Tiered Administration Model?
options: A three-phase deployment model for AD
Organizes AD OUs into three levels
Separates admin privileges into tiers (Tier 0=DC/AD, Tier 1=Servers, Tier 2=Workstations) to limit lateral movement
questions.explanation: The AD Tier Model isolates admin accounts: Tier 0 (DCs/AD only), Tier 1 (servers), Tier 2 (workstations). Admins have separate accounts per tier with no credential reuse. If Tier 2 is compromised, those credentials cannot reach Tier 0.
questions.takeaway: Tiered Admin Model: T0=DC/AD, T1=servers, T2=workstations; separate creds, no crossing tiers

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Infrastructure Master FSMO role?
options: Maintains cross-domain object references, translating GUIDs to names for objects in other domains
Manages the forest infrastructure master record
questions.explanation: The Infrastructure Master updates cross-domain object references (phantoms) — when an object in one domain is a member of a group in another domain. Best practice: do not place it on a GC server (except in single-domain forests).
questions.takeaway: Infrastructure Master = Updates cross-domain object references; avoid placing on GC

questions.question: What is the PrintNightmare vulnerability (CVE-2021-34527)?
options: A critical Print Spooler vulnerability allowing remote code execution and local privilege escalation
A performance issue in print spooler
A Print Spooler denial of service vulnerability
A vulnerability in network printing only
questions.explanation: PrintNightmare (CVE-2021-34527) was a critical vulnerability in Windows Print Spooler (spoolsv.exe) allowing both remote code execution (as SYSTEM) and local privilege escalation. Recommendation: disable Print Spooler on DCs and servers not needing printing.
questions.takeaway: PrintNightmare = Print Spooler RCE+PE; disable spoolsv.exe on DCs and non-print servers

questions.question: What is the difference between ADFS and Azure AD (Entra ID) for federation?
options: ADFS is cloud-based; Azure AD is on-premises
ADFS is on-premises federation using SAML/WS-Fed; Azure AD/Entra ID is Microsoft's cloud identity platform with OAuth2/OIDC support
ADFS replaces Azure AD in hybrid environments
questions.explanation: ADFS (Active Directory Federation Services) provides on-premises SSO using SAML 2.0 and WS-Federation. Azure AD (now Entra ID) is Microsoft's cloud identity platform supporting OAuth 2.0, OIDC, and SAML. Azure AD Connect syncs on-prem AD to Entra ID.
questions.takeaway: ADFS = On-prem SAML/WS-Fed federation; Entra ID = Cloud IdP with OAuth2/OIDC

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Protected Users group Kerberos ticket lifetime restriction?
options: Kerberos tickets are permanent for Protected Users
Protected Users get longer ticket lifetimes
Ticket lifetime is set by the DC regardless of group membership
Kerberos TGTs for Protected Users expire after 4 hours and cannot be renewed
questions.explanation: For Protected Users group members, Kerberos TGTs expire after 4 hours (vs. default 10 hours) and cannot be renewed. This limits the window for ticket-based attacks like Pass-the-Ticket.
questions.takeaway: Protected Users = TGT expires in 4h (not 10h) and cannot be renewed; limits ticket theft

questions.question: What is the purpose of Domain Name Master FSMO role?
options: Controls the addition and removal of domains and application partitions in the forest
Manages DNS records for the domain
Controls the primary domain name resolution
Manages the domain naming convention policy
questions.explanation: The Domain Naming Master (forest-wide FSMO) controls the addition and removal of domains and application directory partitions in the AD forest. It ensures each domain name is unique. Only one exists per forest.
questions.takeaway: Domain Naming Master = Controls domain add/remove in forest; ensures unique domain names

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Schema Master FSMO role?
options: Manages the schema of Group Policy Objects
Controls the Active Directory schema, which defines all object classes and attributes
questions.explanation: The Schema Master (forest-wide FSMO) is the only DC that can make changes to the AD schema — the blueprint defining all object classes (user, computer, group) and their attributes. Schema changes replicate to all DCs in the forest.
questions.takeaway: Schema Master = Only DC that can modify AD schema; forest-wide; one per forest

questions.question: What is RODC (Read-Only Domain Controller) and when is it used?
options: A DC that stores only read permissions
A DC that only replicates from other DCs
A backup domain controller that cannot process logins
A read-only copy of the AD database used in branch offices for security
questions.explanation: An RODC holds a read-only copy of the AD database. It cannot accept write operations (create OUs, add users). Used in branch offices where physical security cannot be guaranteed — a compromised RODC exposes less than a writable DC.
questions.takeaway: RODC = Read-Only DC; cannot create/modify AD objects; used in branch offices for security

questions.question: What is Windows Defender Credential Guard's effect on Kerberos authentication?
options: It isolates LSASS secrets in VBS so even kernel-mode attackers cannot extract Kerberos/NTLM hashes
It replaces Kerberos with a more secure protocol
It requires smart card authentication for all Kerberos tickets
questions.explanation: Credential Guard runs LSASS in an isolated VBS (Virtual Secure Mode) environment. Even kernel-mode malware cannot access the Kerberos ticket cache or NTLM hashes stored there, directly blocking Pass-the-Hash and Pass-the-Ticket attacks.
questions.takeaway: Credential Guard = LSASS runs in VBS; blocks hash/ticket extraction even from kernel-mode malware

questions.question: Which Linux command displays the number of CPU cores available?
questions.explanation: nproc displays the number of processing units (CPU cores/threads) available. 'nproc --all' shows total hardware threads. 'lscpu' gives a more detailed CPU breakdown.
questions.takeaway: nproc = Show number of available CPU cores/threads

questions.question: What is the Windows 'cipher' command used for?
options: Encrypts and decrypts files using AES cipher
Manages EFS encrypted files and displays encryption status of files/folders
Sets the encryption cipher for BitLocker
questions.explanation: The cipher command manages EFS (Encrypting File System) encryption. 'cipher /e file' encrypts a file. 'cipher /d file' decrypts. 'cipher /w:path' securely wipes free space by overwriting it.
questions.takeaway: cipher = Manage EFS encryption; /e encrypt, /d decrypt, /w wipe free space

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'chattr' command in Linux?
options: Changes file attributes like immutable flag to prevent modification even by root
questions.explanation: 'chattr +i file' sets the immutable flag, preventing the file from being modified, deleted, or renamed even by root. 'chattr +a file' sets append-only. 'lsattr' shows these extended attributes.
questions.takeaway: chattr +i = Immutable file (no modify/delete even by root); lsattr shows attributes
